      "           **kwargs):\n        # Set random seed\n        torch.manual_seed(seed)\n        np.random.seed(seed)\n\n        pretrained_model = DISCRIMINATOR_MODELS_PARAMS[discrim][\"pretrained_model\"]\n        print(\"discrim = {}, pretrained_model set \" \"to discriminator's = {}\".format(discrim, pretrained_model))\n\n        # load pretrained model\n        model = GPT2LMHeadModel.from_pretrained(pretrained_model, output_hidden_states=True)\n        model.eval()\n\n        # load tokenizer\n        tokenizer = GPT2Tokenizer.from_pretrained(pretrained_model)\n\n        # Freeze GPT-2 weights\n        for param in model.parameters():\n            param.requires_grad = False\n\n        super().__init__(model=model, tokenizer=tokenizer, **kwargs)\n\n        # Additional setup after creating model and tokenizer\n        self.discrim = discrim\n        classifier = get_classifier(self.discrim, self.device)\n        self.classifier = classifier\n\n    # Default parameters correspond to those in the PPLM paper for the toxicity discriminative model\n    # Others (such as sampling) taken from https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/tree/master/examples/pplm\n    def __call__(self,\n                 cond_text='',\n                 num_samples=1,\n                 class_label=-1,\n                 length=20,\n                 stepsize=0.02,\n                 temperature=1.0,\n      ",
      "              kl_scale=0.01,\n                 repetition_penalty=1.0,\n                 clean_up_tokenization_spaces=True,\n                 include_context_in_generation=False):\n        # Tokenize text\n        tokenized_cond_text = self.tokenizer.encode(self.tokenizer.bos_token + cond_text)\n\n        class_id = get_class_id(self.discrim, class_label)\n\n        pert_gen_tok_texts = []\n        for i in range(num_samples):\n            pert_gen_tok_text, discrim_loss, loss_in_time = generate_text_pplm(\n                model=self.model,\n                context=tokenized_cond_text,\n                device=self.device,\n                perturb=True,\n                classifier=self.classifier,\n                class_label=class_id,\n                length=length,\n                stepsize=stepsize,\n                temperature=temperature,\n                top_k=top_k,\n                sample=sample,\n                num_iterations=num_iterations,\n                grad_length=grad_length,\n       ",
      "      grad = [\n            -stepsize * (p_.grad * window_mask / grad_norms[index] ** gamma).data.cpu().numpy()\n            for index, p_ in enumerate(curr_perturbation)\n        ]\n\n        # accumulate gradient\n        grad_accumulator = list(map(add, grad, grad_accumulator))\n\n        # reset gradients, just to make sure\n        for p_ in curr_perturbation:\n            p_.grad.data.zero_()\n\n        # removing past from the graph\n        new_past = []\n        for p_ in past:\n            new_past.append(p_.detach())\n        past = new_past\n\n    # apply the accumulated perturbations to the past\n    grad_accumulator = [to_var(torch.from_numpy(p_), requires_grad=True, device=device) for p_ in grad_accumulator]\n    pert_past = list(map(add, past, grad_accumulator))\n\n    return pert_past, new_accumulated_hidden, grad_norms, loss_per_iter\n\n\ndef generate_text_pplm(\n        model,\n        context=None,\n        past=None,\n        device=\"cuda\",\n        perturb=True,\n        classifier=None,\n        class_label=None,\n        length=100,\n        stepsize=0.02,\n        temperature=1.0,\n        top_k=10,\n        sample=False,\n        num_iterations=3,\n        grad_length=10000,\n        horizon_length=1,\n        window_length=0,\n        decay=False,\n       ",
      "k=top_k, probs=True)  # + SMALL_CONST\n\n            # rescale\n            if torch.sum(pert_probs) <= 1:\n                pert_probs = pert_probs / torch.sum(pert_probs)\n\n        else:\n            pert_logits = top_k_filter(pert_logits, k=top_k)  # + SMALL_CONST\n            pert_probs = F.softmax(pert_logits, dim=-1)\n\n        # sample or greedy\n        if sample:\n            last = torch.multinomial(pert_probs, num_samples=1)\n\n        else:\n            _, last = torch.topk(pert_probs, k=1, dim=-1)\n\n        # update context/output_so_far appending the new token\n        output_so_far = last if output_so_far is None else torch.cat((output_so_far, last), dim=1)\n\n        # print(tokenizer.decode(output_so_far.tolist()[0]))\n\n    return output_so_far, unpert_discrim_loss, loss_in_time\n\n\nclass PPLMGeneration(Pipeline):\n    def __init__(self,\n                 discrim: str,\n                 seed=0,\n                 **kwargs):\n        # Set random seed\n        torch.manual_seed(seed)\n        np.random.seed(seed)\n\n        pretrained_model = DISCRIMINATOR_MODELS_PARAMS[discrim][\"pretrained_model\"]\n        print(\"discrim = {}, pretrained_model set \" \"to discriminator's = {}\".format(discrim, pretrained_model))\n\n        # load pretrained model\n        model = GPT2LMHeadModel.from_pretrained(pretrained_model, output_hidden_states=True)\n        model.eval()\n\n    "

2025-09-18 23:30:59,531 - llm.llm_def - DEBUG - OpenAI query completed in 19.94s Response: The technical concept describes a training procedure in which the corpus is modified by prepending a toxicity (or control) token before each document so that the model “learns” to condition its generation on that token. In this repository the most direct implementation of this idea appears in the fine‐tuning code for GPT‑2 using control codes. In particular, the file that sets up and manages control codes (via the ctrl_codes option) – which is used to modify the input documents during training – is:

• scripts/finetuning/finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py

Although other files (for example, the experiment runner “scripts/run_prompts_experiment.py”) reference models like “gpt2-ctrl”, those are not directly implementing the conditional pretraining strategy but rather calling models that were trained using that technique. Therefore, the file that directly implements the idea of prepending a toxicity attribute token (i.e. the conditional pretraining) is the training file above.

Final answer:

```
["scripts/finetuning/finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py"]

2. Overview of the Code repository:
# Real Toxicity Prompts Project Overview

## Overview
This repository contains the code and data for the *Real Toxicity Prompts* project by AllenAI, which investigates the propensity of language models to generate toxic content. The project focuses on analyzing toxicity in generated text from models like GPT-2 and GPT-3 when given prompts from real-world sources. Key components include:
- Toxicity scoring via Perspective API
- Text generation using multiple models (GPT-2, PPLM)
- Dataset processing and analysis tools
- Notebooks for exploratory analysis

The project is associated with a [research paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.11462) and has a [demo website](https://toxicdegeneration.allenai.org/). The data is available separately on the AllenAI website.

## System Architecture

### Main Components
1. **Data Processing** (`scripts/data/`):
   - Tools for processing webtext data (`unpack_openwebtext.py`, `process_urls.py`)
   - Dataset creation utilities (`create_prompts_dataset.py`, `create_text_db.py`)
   - Specialized processing (African American English extraction, duplicate detection)

2. **Model Generation** (`generation/`):
   - Base generation framework (`generation.py`)
   - Specific model implementations (GPT-2, PPLM)
   - Notebooks for analysis of generations

3. **Model Training** (`scripts/finetuning/`):
   - Fine-tuning scripts for affect language models
   - Control codes implementation for GPT-2

4. **Evaluation** (`scripts/evaluation/`):
   - Language modeling evaluation on webtext
   - Custom dataset handling

5. **Utilities** (`utils/`):
   - Database interface (`db.py`)
   - Perspective API wrapper (`perspective_api.py`)
   - General utilities and constants

### Workflow
1. Data preparation (webtext processing → prompt dataset creation)
2. Model fine-tuning (optional)
3. Generation experiments (run via `run_prompts_experiment.py`)
4. Analysis (via provided notebooks)

## Core Features

### Data Features
- **Real-world prompt dataset**: Contains prompts from various sources with toxicity annotations
- **Naughty words list**: Curated list of potentially offensive terms (`data/list_of_naughty_and_bad_words.txt`)
- **Webtext processing**: Tools to handle large-scale text data

### Generation Capabilities
- Multiple generation methods:
  - Standard GPT-2 (`gpt2_generation.py`)
  - PPLM (Plug and Play Language Models) variant (`pplm_generation.py`)
- Control mechanisms for generation parameters

### Toxicity Analysis
- Integration with Perspective API for toxicity scoring
- Notebooks for analyzing generation results (`notebooks/realtoxicityprompts_results.ipynb`)
- Comparative analysis tools

### Model Customization
- Fine-tuning scripts for:
  - Affect language models (`finetune_affect_lm.py`)
  - Controlled GPT-2 generation (`finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py`)

## Usage
The project is designed to be run in a Conda environment (`rtp`) with all dependencies specified in `environment.yml`. Main entry points are:
- `scripts/run_prompts_experiment.py` for running generation experiments
- Jupyter notebooks for analysis
- Individual scripts for specific data processing tasks

## Dependencies
Key external dependencies include:
- Perspective API (for toxicity scoring)
- OpenAI API (for GPT-3 access)
- PyTorch (for model implementations)
- Transformers library (for language models)

The project provides a comprehensive framework for studying toxicity in language model generations, with particular attention to real-world scenarios and controlled experiments.

Analysis: The technical concept "Toxicity Representation Learning" involves learning a two‑dimensional representation (toxic vs. non‑toxic) for each token. In the provided repository, the module implemented in models/affect_lm.py defines the core mechanism (with a two‐dimensional affect representation via a linear mapping) that can be interpreted as such a toxicity representation. Its use is further directly handled during model fine-tuning in scripts/finetuning/finetune_affect_lm.py. These two files are directly involved in both the implementation and configuration of the technique as described, whereas the remaining files focus on dataset processing, evaluation, or other aspects not directly tied to learning the token-level toxicity representation....

2. Overview of the Code repository:
# Real Toxicity Prompts Project Overview

## Overview
This repository contains the code and data for the *Real Toxicity Prompts* project by AllenAI, which investigates the propensity of language models to generate toxic content. The project focuses on analyzing toxicity in generated text from models like GPT-2 and GPT-3 when given prompts from real-world sources. Key components include:
- Toxicity scoring via Perspective API
- Text generation using multiple models (GPT-2, PPLM)
- Dataset processing and analysis tools
- Notebooks for exploratory analysis

The project is associated with a [research paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.11462) and has a [demo website](https://toxicdegeneration.allenai.org/). The data is available separately on the AllenAI website.

## System Architecture

### Main Components
1. **Data Processing** (`scripts/data/`):
   - Tools for processing webtext data (`unpack_openwebtext.py`, `process_urls.py`)
   - Dataset creation utilities (`create_prompts_dataset.py`, `create_text_db.py`)
   - Specialized processing (African American English extraction, duplicate detection)

2. **Model Generation** (`generation/`):
   - Base generation framework (`generation.py`)
   - Specific model implementations (GPT-2, PPLM)
   - Notebooks for analysis of generations

3. **Model Training** (`scripts/finetuning/`):
   - Fine-tuning scripts for affect language models
   - Control codes implementation for GPT-2

4. **Evaluation** (`scripts/evaluation/`):
   - Language modeling evaluation on webtext
   - Custom dataset handling

5. **Utilities** (`utils/`):
   - Database interface (`db.py`)
   - Perspective API wrapper (`perspective_api.py`)
   - General utilities and constants

### Workflow
1. Data preparation (webtext processing → prompt dataset creation)
2. Model fine-tuning (optional)
3. Generation experiments (run via `run_prompts_experiment.py`)
4. Analysis (via provided notebooks)

## Core Features

### Data Features
- **Real-world prompt dataset**: Contains prompts from various sources with toxicity annotations
- **Naughty words list**: Curated list of potentially offensive terms (`data/list_of_naughty_and_bad_words.txt`)
- **Webtext processing**: Tools to handle large-scale text data

### Generation Capabilities
- Multiple generation methods:
  - Standard GPT-2 (`gpt2_generation.py`)
  - PPLM (Plug and Play Language Models) variant (`pplm_generation.py`)
- Control mechanisms for generation parameters

### Toxicity Analysis
- Integration with Perspective API for toxicity scoring
- Notebooks for analyzing generation results (`notebooks/realtoxicityprompts_results.ipynb`)
- Comparative analysis tools

### Model Customization
- Fine-tuning scripts for:
  - Affect language models (`finetune_affect_lm.py`)
  - Controlled GPT-2 generation (`finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py`)

## Usage
The project is designed to be run in a Conda environment (`rtp`) with all dependencies specified in `environment.yml`. Main entry points are:
- `scripts/run_prompts_experiment.py` for running generation experiments
- Jupyter notebooks for analysis
- Individual scripts for specific data processing tasks

## Dependencies
Key external dependencies include:
- Perspective API (for toxicity scoring)
- OpenAI API (for GPT-3 access)
- PyTorch (for model implementations)
- Transformers library (for language models)

The project provides a comprehensive framework for studying toxicity in language model generations, with particular attention to real-world scenarios and controlled experiments.

    "file_name": "models/affect_lm.py",
    "code_list": [
      "from typing import Optional\n\nimport torch\nfrom torch import nn\nfrom torch.nn import CrossEntropyLoss\nfrom transformers import GPT2LMHeadModel\n\nNUM_AFFECTS = 2\n\n\nclass Affect(nn.Module):\n    \"\"\"\n    See section 4.3 of Ghosh:\n        Affect-LM can also be used as a language model where the next predicted word is\n        estimated from the words in the context, along with an affect category extracted\n        from the context words themselves (instead of being encoded externally as in generation).\n\n    beta in [1.0, 1.5, 1.75, 2.0, 2.25, 2.5, 3.0]\n    \"\"\"\n\n    IGNORE_IDS = [50256, 247, 251, 250]\n\n    def __init__(self,\n                 affect_dim: int,\n                 vocab_size: int,\n                 beta: float = 1.0,\n                 ignore_special_tokens=False):\n        super().__init__()\n        self.beta = beta\n        self.ignore_special_tokens = ignore_special_tokens\n        self.affect2vocab = nn.Linear(affect_dim, vocab_size, bias=True)\n        self.init_weights()\n\n    def forward(self, affect_labels: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:\n        out = self.affect2vocab(affect_labels)\n        if self.ignore_special_tokens:\n            out[:, :, self.IGNORE_IDS] = 0\n        return self.beta * out\n\n    def init_weights(self, initializer_range=0.02):\n        # initializer_range taken from GPT2Config\n        self.affect2vocab.weight.data.normal_(mean=0.0, std=initializer_range)\n        self.affect2vocab.bias.data.zero_()\n\n\nclass AffectGPT2LMHeadModel(GPT2LMHeadModel):\n    affect_labels: Optional[torch.Tensor]\n\n    def __init__(self, config):\n       "
    ]

** gm_scale) * (unpert_probs ** (1 - gm_scale))  # + SMALL_CONST\\n            pert_probs = top_k_filter(pert_probs, k=top_k, probs=True)  # + SMALL_CONST\\n\\n            # rescale\\n            if probability tokens)\\n                    if temperature != 1.0:\\n                        next_token_logits = next_token_logits / temperature\\n                    # Top-p/top-k filtering\\n 2025-09-18 23:31:44,986 - llm.llm_def - DEBUG - OpenAI query completed in 26.56s Response: Based on the review, the only part of the repository that implements an explicit mechanism for “logit adjustment” is the module where the vocabulary logits are modified via an extra term (scaled by β) before being used for generation. In our repository this is done in the affect‐based model. In particular:

1. In models/affect_lm.py the Affect layer is defined. Its forward() call computes an extra logit term (via a linear mapping of an “affect” signal) and scales it by β. This is the direct implementation of an adjustment to the logits.

2. In scripts/finetuning/finetune_affect_lm.py the training is set up for the AffectLM so that the logit‐adjustment mechanism is learned. Although it does not compute the adjustment itself (it relies on the module from models/affect_lm.py), it directly configures and fine‐tunes the model that uses that adjustment during generation.

3. In scripts/run_prompts_experiment.py one branch is dedicated to the “gpt2-affect” model variant that will (under the hood) employ the affect module (and thus the logit adjustment) during generation.

Thus, the files directly relevant to this particular technical concept – and listed here in descending order of relevance – are:

• models/affect_lm.py  
• scripts/finetuning/finetune_affect_lm.py  
• scripts/run_prompts_experiment.py

If no file had implemented the described method, we would have returned None; however, since the Affect module provides a direct implementation, the answer is as above.

2. Overview of the Code repository:
# Real Toxicity Prompts Project Overview

## Overview
This repository contains the code and data for the *Real Toxicity Prompts* project by AllenAI, which investigates the propensity of language models to generate toxic content. The project focuses on analyzing toxicity in generated text from models like GPT-2 and GPT-3 when given prompts from real-world sources. Key components include:
- Toxicity scoring via Perspective API
- Text generation using multiple models (GPT-2, PPLM)
- Dataset processing and analysis tools
- Notebooks for exploratory analysis

The project is associated with a [research paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.11462) and has a [demo website](https://toxicdegeneration.allenai.org/). The data is available separately on the AllenAI website.

## System Architecture

### Main Components
1. **Data Processing** (`scripts/data/`):
   - Tools for processing webtext data (`unpack_openwebtext.py`, `process_urls.py`)
   - Dataset creation utilities (`create_prompts_dataset.py`, `create_text_db.py`)
   - Specialized processing (African American English extraction, duplicate detection)

2. **Model Generation** (`generation/`):
   - Base generation framework (`generation.py`)
   - Specific model implementations (GPT-2, PPLM)
   - Notebooks for analysis of generations

3. **Model Training** (`scripts/finetuning/`):
   - Fine-tuning scripts for affect language models
   - Control codes implementation for GPT-2

4. **Evaluation** (`scripts/evaluation/`):
   - Language modeling evaluation on webtext
   - Custom dataset handling

5. **Utilities** (`utils/`):
   - Database interface (`db.py`)
   - Perspective API wrapper (`perspective_api.py`)
   - General utilities and constants

### Workflow
1. Data preparation (webtext processing → prompt dataset creation)
2. Model fine-tuning (optional)
3. Generation experiments (run via `run_prompts_experiment.py`)
4. Analysis (via provided notebooks)

## Core Features

### Data Features
- **Real-world prompt dataset**: Contains prompts from various sources with toxicity annotations
- **Naughty words list**: Curated list of potentially offensive terms (`data/list_of_naughty_and_bad_words.txt`)
- **Webtext processing**: Tools to handle large-scale text data

### Generation Capabilities
- Multiple generation methods:
  - Standard GPT-2 (`gpt2_generation.py`)
  - PPLM (Plug and Play Language Models) variant (`pplm_generation.py`)
- Control mechanisms for generation parameters

### Toxicity Analysis
- Integration with Perspective API for toxicity scoring
- Notebooks for analyzing generation results (`notebooks/realtoxicityprompts_results.ipynb`)
- Comparative analysis tools

### Model Customization
- Fine-tuning scripts for:
  - Affect language models (`finetune_affect_lm.py`)
  - Controlled GPT-2 generation (`finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py`)

## Usage
The project is designed to be run in a Conda environment (`rtp`) with all dependencies specified in `environment.yml`. Main entry points are:
- `scripts/run_prompts_experiment.py` for running generation experiments
- Jupyter notebooks for analysis
- Individual scripts for specific data processing tasks

## Dependencies
Key external dependencies include:
- Perspective API (for toxicity scoring)
- OpenAI API (for GPT-3 access)
- PyTorch (for model implementations)
- Transformers library (for language models)

The project provides a comprehensive framework for studying toxicity in language model generations, with particular attention to real-world scenarios and controlled experiments.

This is a very ugly\n        # way that works at the moment of setting the pad_token_id to the <EOS> token that is already\n        # included in the vocab size.\n        self.tokenizer = GPT2Tokenizer.from_pretrained(tokenizer, pad_token=self.STOP_TOKEN)\n        assert ",
      " # IMPORTANT: Note that setting the pad token like this in the constructor gives the pad_token the\n        # pad_token_id = 50256, which normally belongs to the <EOS> token_id in GPT2. This is a very ugly\n        # way that works at the moment of setting the pad_token_id to the <EOS> token that is already\n        # included in the vocab size.\n        self.tokenizer = GPT2Tokenizer.from_pretrained(tokenizer, pad_token=self.STOP_TOKEN)\n        assert self.tokenizer.eos_token_id == self.tokenizer.pad_token_id\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return f'<GPT2Generator model_name_or_path=\"{self.model}\">'\n\n    def __call__(self, *args, **kwargs):\n        return self.generate(*args, **kwargs)\n\n    def generate(self,\n                 prompt: Union[str, List[str]],\n                 max_len: int = 20,\n                 sample: bool = True,\n                 k: int = 0,\n                 p: float = 0.9,\n                 temperature: float = 1.0,\n                 bad_words_ids: List[List[int]] = None,\n                 **model_kwargs) -> List[str]:\n        if isinstance(prompt, str):\n            prompt = [prompt]\n\n        encodings_dict = self.tokenizer.batch_encode_plus(prompt, pad_to_max_length=True, return_tensors='pt')\n\n        input_ids = encodings_dict['input_ids'].to(self.device)\n       ",
      "     temperature: float = 1.0,\n                 bad_words_ids: List[List[int]] = None,\n                 **model_kwargs) -> List[str]:\n        if isinstance(prompt, str):\n            prompt = [prompt]\n\n        encodings_dict = self.tokenizer.batch_encode_plus(prompt, pad_to_max_length=True, return_tensors='pt')\n\n        input_ids = encodings_dict['input_ids'].to(self.device)\n        attention_mask = encodings_dict['attention_mask'].to(self.device)\n        batch_size, input_seq_len = input_ids.shape\n\n        position_ids = attention_mask.cumsum(dim=1) - 1\n        unfinished_sents = torch.ones(batch_size, dtype=torch.long, device=self.device)\n\n        self.model.eval()\n        with torch.no_grad():\n            for step in range(max_len):\n                logits, past = self.model(input_ids, attention_mask=attention_mask, position_ids=position_ids,\n                                          **model_kwargs)\n\n                # in the first decoding step, we want to use the 'real' last position for each sentence\n                if step == 0:\n                    last_non_masked_idx = torch.sum(attention_mask, dim=1) - 1\n                    next_token_logits = logits[range(batch_size), last_non_masked_idx, :]\n                else:\n ",
      "                   # Greedy decoding\n                    next_tokens = torch.argmax(next_token_logits, dim=-1)\n\n                # either append a padding token here if <EOS> has been seen or append next token\n                tokens_to_add = next_tokens * unfinished_sents + self.tokenizer.pad_token_id * (1 - unfinished_sents)\n\n                # this updates which sentences have not seen an EOS token so far\n                # if one EOS token was seen the sentence is finished\n                eos_in_sents = tokens_to_add == self.tokenizer.eos_token_id\n                unfinished_sents.mul_((~eos_in_sents).long())\n\n                # stop when there is an EOS in each sentence\n                if unfinished_sents.max() == 0:\n                    break\n\n                # Update input_ids, attention_mask and position_ids\n                input_ids = torch.cat([input_ids, tokens_to_add.unsqueeze(-1)], dim=-1)\n                attention_mask = torch.cat([attention_mask, attention_mask.new_ones((batch_size, 1))], dim=1)\n                position_ids = torch.cat([position_ids, (position_ids[:, -1] + 1).unsqueeze(-1)], dim=1)\n\n        decoded_outputs = "

Data preparation (webtext processing → prompt dataset creation)\n2. Model fine-tuning (optional)\n3. Generation experiments (run via `run_prompts_experiment.py`)\n4. Analysis (via provided notebooks)\n\n## Core Features\n\n### Data Features\n- **Real-world prompt dataset**: Contains prompts from various sources with toxicity annotations\n- **Naughty words list**: Curated list of potentially offensive terms (`data/list_of_naughty_and_bad_words.txt`)\n- **Webtext processing**: Tools to handle large-scale text data\n\n### Generation Capabilities\n- Multiple generation methods:\n  - Standard GPT-2 (`gpt2_generation.py`)\n  - PPLM (Plug and Play Language Models) variant (`pplm_generation.py`)\n- Control mechanisms for generation parameters\n\n### Toxicity Analysis\n- Integration with Perspective API for toxicity scoring\n- Notebooks for analyzing generation results (`notebooks/realtoxicityprompts_results.ipynb`)\n- Comparative analysis tools\n\n### Model Customization\n- Fine-tuning scripts for:\n  - Affect language models (`finetune_affect_lm.py`)\n  - Controlled GPT-2 generation (`finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py`)\n\n## Usage\nThe project is designed to be run in a Conda environment (`rtp`) with all dependencies specified in `environment.yml`. Main entry points are:\n- `scripts/run_prompts_experiment.py` for running generation experiments\n- Jupyter notebooks for analysis\n- Individual scripts for specific data processing tasks\n\n## Dependencies\nKey external dependencies include:\n- Perspective API (for toxicity scoring)\n- OpenAI API (for GPT-3 access)\n- PyTorch (for model implementations)\n- Transformers library (for language models)\n\nThe project provides a comprehensive framework for studying toxicity in language model generations, with particular attention to real-world scenarios and controlled experiments.\n\n3. 2. Overview of the Code repository:
# Real Toxicity Prompts Project Overview

## Overview
This repository contains the code and data for the *Real Toxicity Prompts* project by AllenAI, which investigates the propensity of language models to generate toxic content. The project focuses on analyzing toxicity in generated text from models like GPT-2 and GPT-3 when given prompts from real-world sources. Key components include:
- Toxicity scoring via Perspective API
- Text generation using multiple models (GPT-2, PPLM)
- Dataset processing and analysis tools
- Notebooks for exploratory analysis

The project is associated with a [research paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.11462) and has a [demo website](https://toxicdegeneration.allenai.org/). The data is available separately on the AllenAI website.

## System Architecture

### Main Components
1. **Data Processing** (`scripts/data/`):
   - Tools for processing webtext data (`unpack_openwebtext.py`, `process_urls.py`)
   - Dataset creation utilities (`create_prompts_dataset.py`, `create_text_db.py`)
   - Specialized processing (African American English extraction, duplicate detection)

2. **Model Generation** (`generation/`):
   - Base generation framework (`generation.py`)
   - Specific model implementations (GPT-2, PPLM)
   - Notebooks for analysis of generations

3. **Model Training** (`scripts/finetuning/`):
   - Fine-tuning scripts for affect language models
   - Control codes implementation for GPT-2

4. **Evaluation** (`scripts/evaluation/`):
   - Language modeling evaluation on webtext
   - Custom dataset handling

5. **Utilities** (`utils/`):
   - Database interface (`db.py`)
   - Perspective API wrapper (`perspective_api.py`)
   - General utilities and constants

### Workflow
1. Data preparation (webtext processing → prompt dataset creation)
2. Model fine-tuning (optional)
3. Generation experiments (run via `run_prompts_experiment.py`)
4. Analysis (via provided notebooks)

## Core Features

### Data Features
- **Real-world prompt dataset**: Contains prompts from various sources with toxicity annotations
- **Naughty words list**: Curated list of potentially offensive terms (`data/list_of_naughty_and_bad_words.txt`)
- **Webtext processing**: Tools to handle large-scale text data

### Generation Capabilities
- Multiple generation methods:
  - Standard GPT-2 (`gpt2_generation.py`)
  - PPLM (Plug and Play Language Models) variant (`pplm_generation.py`)
- Control mechanisms for generation parameters

### Toxicity Analysis
- Integration with Perspective API for toxicity scoring
- Notebooks for analyzing generation results (`notebooks/realtoxicityprompts_results.ipynb`)
- Comparative analysis tools

### Model Customization
- Fine-tuning scripts for:
  - Affect language models (`finetune_affect_lm.py`)
  - Controlled GPT-2 generation (`finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py`)

## Usage
The project is designed to be run in a Conda environment (`rtp`) with all dependencies specified in `environment.yml`. Main entry points are:
- `scripts/run_prompts_experiment.py` for running generation experiments
- Jupyter notebooks for analysis
- Individual scripts for specific data processing tasks

## Dependencies
Key external dependencies include:
- Perspective API (for toxicity scoring)
- OpenAI API (for GPT-3 access)
- PyTorch (for model implementations)
- Transformers library (for language models)

The project provides a comprehensive framework for studying toxicity in language model generations, with particular attention to real-world scenarios and controlled experiments.

      "k=top_k, probs=True)  # + SMALL_CONST\n\n            # rescale\n            if torch.sum(pert_probs) <= 1:\n                pert_probs = pert_probs / torch.sum(pert_probs)\n\n        else:\n            pert_logits = top_k_filter(pert_logits, k=top_k)  # + SMALL_CONST\n            pert_probs = F.softmax(pert_logits, dim=-1)\n\n        # sample or greedy\n        if sample:\n            last = torch.multinomial(pert_probs, num_samples=1)\n\n        else:\n            _, last = torch.topk(pert_probs, k=1, dim=-1)\n\n        # update context/output_so_far appending the new token\n        output_so_far = last if output_so_far is None else torch.cat((output_so_far, last), dim=1)\n\n        # print(tokenizer.decode(output_so_far.tolist()[0]))\n\n    return output_so_far, unpert_discrim_loss, loss_in_time\n\n\nclass PPLMGeneration(Pipeline):\n    def __init__(self,\n                 discrim: str,\n                 seed=0,\n                 **kwargs):\n        # Set random seed\n        torch.manual_seed(seed)\n        np.random.seed(seed)\n\n        pretrained_model = DISCRIMINATOR_MODELS_PARAMS[discrim][\"pretrained_model\"]\n        print(\"discrim = {}, pretrained_model set \" \"to discriminator's = {}\".format(discrim, pretrained_model))\n\n        # load pretrained model\n        model = GPT2LMHeadModel.from_pretrained(pretrained_model, output_hidden_states=True)\n        model.eval()\n\n    ",
      "     classifier=None,\n        class_label=None,\n        length=100,\n        stepsize=0.02,\n        temperature=1.0,\n        top_k=10,\n        sample=False,\n        num_iterations=3,\n        grad_length=10000,\n        horizon_length=1,\n        window_length=0,\n        decay=False,\n        gamma=1.5,\n        gm_scale=0.9,\n        kl_scale=0.01,\n        repetition_penalty=1.0,\n):\n    output_so_far = None\n    if context:\n        context_t = torch.tensor(context, device=device, dtype=torch.long)\n        while len(context_t.shape) < 2:\n            context_t = context_t.unsqueeze(0)\n        output_so_far = context_t\n\n    grad_norms = None\n    last = None\n    unpert_discrim_loss = 0\n    loss_in_time = []\n    for i in trange(length, desc='Generating with PPLM', disable=True):\n\n        # Get past/probs for current output, except for last word\n        # Note that GPT takes 2 inputs: past + current_token\n\n        # run model forward to obtain unperturbed\n        if past is None and output_so_far is not None:\n            last = output_so_far[:, -1:]\n            if output_so_far.shape[1] > 1:\n                _, past, _ = model(output_so_far[:, :-1])\n\n        unpert_logits, unpert_past, unpert_all_hidden = model(output_so_far)\n     ",
      "      grad = [\n            -stepsize * (p_.grad * window_mask / grad_norms[index] ** gamma).data.cpu().numpy()\n            for index, p_ in enumerate(curr_perturbation)\n        ]\n\n        # accumulate gradient\n        grad_accumulator = list(map(add, grad, grad_accumulator))\n\n        # reset gradients, just to make sure\n        for p_ in curr_perturbation:\n            p_.grad.data.zero_()\n\n        # removing past from the graph\n        new_past = []\n        for p_ in past:\n            new_past.append(p_.detach())\n        past = new_past\n\n    # apply the accumulated perturbations to the past\n    grad_accumulator = [to_var(torch.from_numpy(p_), requires_grad=True, device=device) for p_ in grad_accumulator]\n    pert_past = list(map(add, past, grad_accumulator))\n\n    return pert_past, new_accumulated_hidden, grad_norms, loss_per_iter\n\n\ndef generate_text_pplm(\n        model,\n        context=None,\n        past=None,\n        device=\"cuda\",\n        perturb=True,\n        classifier=None,\n        class_label=None,\n        length=100,\n        stepsize=0.02,\n        temperature=1.0,\n        top_k=10,\n        sample=False,\n        num_iterations=3,\n        grad_length=10000,\n        horizon_length=1,\n        window_length=0,\n        decay=False,\n       "

2. Overview of the Code repository:
# Real Toxicity Prompts Project Overview

## Overview
This repository contains the code and data for the *Real Toxicity Prompts* project by AllenAI, which investigates the propensity of language models to generate toxic content. The project focuses on analyzing toxicity in generated text from models like GPT-2 and GPT-3 when given prompts from real-world sources. Key components include:
- Toxicity scoring via Perspective API
- Text generation using multiple models (GPT-2, PPLM)
- Dataset processing and analysis tools
- Notebooks for exploratory analysis

The project is associated with a [research paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.11462) and has a [demo website](https://toxicdegeneration.allenai.org/). The data is available separately on the AllenAI website.

## System Architecture

### Main Components
1. **Data Processing** (`scripts/data/`):
   - Tools for processing webtext data (`unpack_openwebtext.py`, `process_urls.py`)
   - Dataset creation utilities (`create_prompts_dataset.py`, `create_text_db.py`)
   - Specialized processing (African American English extraction, duplicate detection)

2. **Model Generation** (`generation/`):
   - Base generation framework (`generation.py`)
   - Specific model implementations (GPT-2, PPLM)
   - Notebooks for analysis of generations

3. **Model Training** (`scripts/finetuning/`):
   - Fine-tuning scripts for affect language models
   - Control codes implementation for GPT-2

4. **Evaluation** (`scripts/evaluation/`):
   - Language modeling evaluation on webtext
   - Custom dataset handling

5. **Utilities** (`utils/`):
   - Database interface (`db.py`)
   - Perspective API wrapper (`perspective_api.py`)
   - General utilities and constants

### Workflow
1. Data preparation (webtext processing → prompt dataset creation)
2. Model fine-tuning (optional)
3. Generation experiments (run via `run_prompts_experiment.py`)
4. Analysis (via provided notebooks)

## Core Features

### Data Features
- **Real-world prompt dataset**: Contains prompts from various sources with toxicity annotations
- **Naughty words list**: Curated list of potentially offensive terms (`data/list_of_naughty_and_bad_words.txt`)
- **Webtext processing**: Tools to handle large-scale text data

### Generation Capabilities
- Multiple generation methods:
  - Standard GPT-2 (`gpt2_generation.py`)
  - PPLM (Plug and Play Language Models) variant (`pplm_generation.py`)
- Control mechanisms for generation parameters

### Toxicity Analysis
- Integration with Perspective API for toxicity scoring
- Notebooks for analyzing generation results (`notebooks/realtoxicityprompts_results.ipynb`)
- Comparative analysis tools

### Model Customization
- Fine-tuning scripts for:
  - Affect language models (`finetune_affect_lm.py`)
  - Controlled GPT-2 generation (`finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py`)

## Usage
The project is designed to be run in a Conda environment (`rtp`) with all dependencies specified in `environment.yml`. Main entry points are:
- `scripts/run_prompts_experiment.py` for running generation experiments
- Jupyter notebooks for analysis
- Individual scripts for specific data processing tasks

## Dependencies
Key external dependencies include:
- Perspective API (for toxicity scoring)
- OpenAI API (for GPT-3 access)
- PyTorch (for model implementations)
- Transformers library (for language models)

The project provides a comprehensive framework for studying toxicity in language model generations, with particular attention to real-world scenarios and controlled experiments.

\"class_vocab\": {\"non_toxic\": 0, \"toxic\": 1},\n        \"default_class\": 0,\n        \"pretrained_model\": \"gpt2-medium\",\n    }\n}\n\n\ndef to_var(x, requires_grad=False, volatile=False, device=\"cuda\"):\n    if torch.cuda.is_available() and device == \"cuda\":\n        x = x.cuda()\n    elif device != \"cuda\":\n        x = x.to(device)\n    return Variable(x, requires_grad=requires_grad, volatile=volatile)\n\n\ndef top_k_filter(logits, k, probs=False):\n    \"\"\"\n    Masks everything but the k top entries as -infinity (1e10).\n    Used to mask logits such that e^-infinity -> 0 won't contribute to the\n    sum of the denominator.\n    \"\"\"\n    if k == 0:\n        return logits\n    else:\n        values = torch.topk(logits, k)[0]\n        batch_mins = values[:, -1].view(-1, 1).expand_as(logits)\n        if probs:\n            return torch.where(logits < batch_mins, torch.ones_like(logits) * 0.0, logits)\n        return torch.where(logits < batch_mins, torch.ones_like(logits) * -BIG_CONST, logits)\n\n\ndef get_classifier(name: str, device: str) -> ClassificationHead:\n    params = DISCRIMINATOR_MODELS_PARAMS[name]\n       "+ 1 + horizon_length))\n\n        label = torch.tensor(prediction.shape[0] * [class_label], device=device, dtype=torch.long)\n        discrim_loss = ce_loss(prediction, label)\n        loss += discrim_loss\n        loss_list.append(discrim_loss)\n\n        if kl_scale > 0.0:\n            unpert_probs = F.softmax(unpert_logits[:, -1, :], dim=-1)\n            unpert_probs = unpert_probs + SMALL_CONST * (unpert_probs <= SMALL_CONST).float().to(device).detach()\n            correction = SMALL_CONST * (probs <= SMALL_CONST).float().to(device).detach()\n            corrected_probs = probs + correction.detach()\n            kl_loss = kl_scale * ((corrected_probs * (corrected_probs / unpert_probs).log()).sum())\n            loss += kl_loss\n\n        loss_per_iter.append(loss.data.cpu().numpy())\n\n        # compute gradients\n        loss.backward()\n\n        # calculate gradient norms\n        grad_norms = [\n            (torch.norm(p_.grad * window_mask) + SMALL_CONST) for index, p_ in enumerate(curr_perturbation)\n        ]\n\n        # normalize gradients\n        grad = [\n            -stepsize * (p_.grad * window_mask / grad_norms[index] ** gamma).data.cpu().numpy()\n            for index, p_ in enumerate(curr_perturbation)\n        ]\n\n        # accumulate gradient\n        grad_accumulator = list(map(add, grad, grad_accumulator))\n\n        # reset gradients, just to make sure\n        for p_ in curr_perturbation:\n ",

      "    ]\n\n        # Compute hidden using perturbed past\n        perturbed_past = list(map(add, past, curr_perturbation))\n        _, _, _, curr_length, _ = curr_perturbation[0].shape\n        all_logits, _, all_hidden = model(last, past=perturbed_past)\n        hidden = all_hidden[-1]\n        new_accumulated_hidden = accumulated_hidden + torch.sum(hidden, dim=1).detach()\n        logits = all_logits[:, -1, :]\n        probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)\n\n        loss = 0.0\n        loss_list = []\n\n        ce_loss = torch.nn.CrossEntropyLoss()\n        curr_unpert_past = unpert_past\n        curr_probs = torch.unsqueeze(probs, dim=1)\n        wte = model.resize_token_embeddings()\n        for _ in range(horizon_length):\n            inputs_embeds = torch.matmul(curr_probs, wte.weight.data)\n            _, curr_unpert_past, curr_all_hidden = model(past=curr_unpert_past, inputs_embeds=inputs_embeds)\n            curr_hidden = curr_all_hidden[-1]\n            new_accumulated_hidden = new_accumulated_hidden + torch.sum(curr_hidden, dim=1)\n\n        prediction = classifier(new_accumulated_hidden / (curr_length + 1 + horizon_length))\n\n        label = torch.tensor(prediction.shape[0] * [class_label], device=device, dtype=torch.long)\n        discrim_loss = ce_loss(prediction, label)\n        loss += discrim_loss\n        loss_list.append(discrim_loss)\n\n        if kl_scale > 0.0:\n            unpert_probs = F.softmax(unpert_logits[:, -1, :], dim=-1)\n            unpert_probs = unpert_probs + SMALL_CONST * (unpert_probs <= SMALL_CONST).float().to(device).detach()\n      ",
      "     classifier=None,\n        class_label=None,\n        length=100,\n        stepsize=0.02,\n        temperature=1.0,\n        top_k=10,\n        sample=False,\n        num_iterations=3,\n        grad_length=10000,\n        horizon_length=1,\n        window_length=0,\n        decay=False,\n        gamma=1.5,\n        gm_scale=0.9,\n        kl_scale=0.01,\n        repetition_penalty=1.0,\n):\n    output_so_far = None\n    if context:\n        context_t = torch.tensor(context, device=device, dtype=torch.long)\n        while len(context_t.shape) < 2:\n            context_t = context_t.unsqueeze(0)\n        output_so_far = context_t\n\n    grad_norms = None\n    last = None\n    unpert_discrim_loss = 0\n    loss_in_time = []\n    for i in trange(length, desc='Generating with PPLM', disable=True):\n\n        # Get past/probs for current output, except for last word\n        # Note that GPT takes 2 inputs: past + current_token\n\n        # run model forward to obtain unperturbed\n        if past is None and output_so_far is not None:\n            last = output_so_far[:, -1:]\n            if output_so_far.shape[1] > 1:\n                _, past, _ = model(output_so_far[:, :-1])\n\n        unpert_logits, unpert_past, unpert_all_hidden = model(output_so_far)\n     ",
      "       num_iterations=num_iterations,\n                    horizon_length=horizon_length,\n                    window_length=window_length,\n                    decay=decay,\n                    gamma=gamma,\n                    kl_scale=kl_scale,\n                    device=device,\n                )\n                loss_in_time.append(loss_this_iter)\n            else:\n                pert_past = past\n\n        pert_logits, past, pert_all_hidden = model(last, past=pert_past)\n        pert_logits = pert_logits[:, -1, :] / temperature  # + SMALL_CONST\n\n        for token_idx in set(output_so_far[0].tolist()):\n            if pert_logits[0, token_idx] < 0:\n                pert_logits[0, token_idx] *= repetition_penalty\n            else:\n                pert_logits[0, token_idx] /= repetition_penalty\n\n        pert_probs = F.softmax(pert_logits, dim=-1)\n\n        if classifier is not None:\n            ce_loss = torch.nn.CrossEntropyLoss()\n            prediction = classifier(torch.mean(unpert_last_hidden, dim=1))\n ",
      "      grad = [\n            -stepsize * (p_.grad * window_mask / grad_norms[index] ** gamma).data.cpu().numpy()\n            for index, p_ in enumerate(curr_perturbation)\n        ]\n\n        # accumulate gradient\n        grad_accumulator = list(map(add, grad, grad_accumulator))\n\n        # reset gradients, just to make sure\n        for p_ in curr_perturbation:\n            p_.grad.data.zero_()\n\n        # removing past from the graph\n        new_past = []\n        for p_ in past:\n            new_past.append(p_.detach())\n        past = new_past\n\n    # apply the accumulated perturbations to the past\n    grad_accumulator = [to_var(torch.from_numpy(p_), requires_grad=True, device=device) for p_ in grad_accumulator]\n    pert_past = list(map(add, past, grad_accumulator))\n\n    return pert_past, new_accumulated_hidden, grad_norms, loss_per_iter\n\n\ndef generate_text_pplm(\n        model,\n        context=None,\n        past=None,\n        device=\"cuda\",\n        perturb=True,\n        classifier=None,\n        class_label=None,\n        length=100,\n        stepsize=0.02,\n        temperature=1.0,\n        top_k=10,\n        sample=False,\n        num_iterations=3,\n        grad_length=10000,\n        horizon_length=1,\n        window_length=0,\n        decay=False,\n       ",
      "           **kwargs):\n        # Set random seed\n        torch.manual_seed(seed)\n        np.random.seed(seed)\n\n        pretrained_model = DISCRIMINATOR_MODELS_PARAMS[discrim][\"pretrained_model\"]\n        print(\"discrim = {}, pretrained_model set \" \"to discriminator's = {}\".format(discrim, pretrained_model))\n\n        # load pretrained model\n        model = GPT2LMHeadModel.from_pretrained(pretrained_model, output_hidden_states=True)\n        model.eval()\n\n        # load tokenizer\n        tokenizer = GPT2Tokenizer.from_pretrained(pretrained_model)\n\n        # Freeze GPT-2 weights\n        for param in model.parameters():\n            param.requires_grad = False\n\n        super().__init__(model=model, tokenizer=tokenizer, **kwargs)\n\n        # Additional setup after creating model and tokenizer\n        self.discrim = discrim\n        classifier = get_classifier(self.discrim, self.device)\n        self.classifier = classifier\n\n    # Default parameters correspond to those in the PPLM paper for the toxicity discriminative model\n    # Others (such as sampling) taken from https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/tree/master/examples/pplm\n    def __call__(self,\n                 cond_text='',\n                 num_samples=1,\n                 class_label=-1,\n                 length=20,\n                 stepsize=0.02,\n                 temperature=1.0,\n      "

      "code": "[{\"file_name\": \"scripts/data/create_prompts_dataset.py\", \"code_list\": [\"from pathlib import Path\\nfrom typing import Union\\n\\nimport pandas as pd\\nimport spacy\\nfrom spacy.tokens.doc import Doc\\nfrom sqlalchemy.sql.functions import random\\n\\nfrom utils.constants import TEXTS_DIR, OPENWEBTEXT_DB\\nfrom utils.db import SpanScore, corpus_db_session\\nimport click\\n\\n# Span constants\\nMIN_SPAN_LEN = 64\\nMAX_SPAN_LEN = 1024\\nMAX_PROMPT_LEN = 128\\n\\n\\ndef split_prompt(doc: Doc, n: int):\\n    if isinstance(n, float):\\n        # TODO: round n rather than flooring it\\n        n = round(n * len(doc))\\n\\n    # Split text into prompt and continuation\\n    prompt = str(doc[:n])\\n    continuation = str(doc)[len(prompt):]  # Rather than taking remaining tokens, take the remainder of the string\\n    if len(prompt) == 0 or len(continuation) == 0 or len(prompt) > MAX_PROMPT_LEN:\\n        return None\\n\\n    return prompt, continuation\\n\\n\\ndef load_span_example(row: pd.Series, n: Union[int, float], nlp):\\n    # Load text from file\\n    text_file = TEXTS_DIR / row.filename\\n    try:\\n        text = text_file.read_text(encoding='utf-8', errors='strict')\\n    except UnicodeDecodeError:\\n        return None\\n\\n    # Trim text\\n    text = text[row.begin:row.end].strip()\\n    if not (MIN_SPAN_LEN <= len(text) <= MAX_SPAN_LEN):\\n        return None\\n\\n    # Tokenize text\\n    doc = nlp(text)\\n    return split_prompt(doc, n)\\n\\n\\n@click.command()\\n@click.option('--out_file', required=True, type=str)\\n@click.option('--n', required=True, type=float)\\ndef create_prompts_dataset(out_file: str, n: float):\\n    out_file = Path(out_file)\\n    if out_file.exists():\\n        raise FileExistsError(\\\"Output file already exists.\\\")\\n    if not OPENWEBTEXT_DB.exists():\\n        raise FileNotFoundError(\\\"Perspective database was not found.\\\")\\n\\n    session = corpus_db_session()\\n\\n    # TODO: do this for all four ranges of toxicity\\n    query = (\\n        session.query(SpanScore)\\n            .filter(SpanScore.toxicity < 0.25)\\n            .filter(SpanScore.end - SpanScore.begin >= MIN_SPAN_LEN)\\n            .filter(SpanScore.end - SpanScore.begin <= MAX_SPAN_LEN)\\n            .order_by(random())\\n            .limit(30_000)\\n    )\\n\\n    # Load dataframe from query and select relevant columns\\n    print(\\\"Reading from database...\\\")\\n    df = pd.read_sql(query.statement, con=query.session.bind)\\n    df = df[['filename', 'begin', 'end', 'toxicity']]\\n    print(f\\\"Returned {len(df)} rows\\\")\\n\\n    # Get prompts and continuations\\n    print(\\\"Loading text and tokenizing...\\\")\\n    nlp = spacy.load('en_core_web_sm', disable=['parser', 'ner', 'tagger'])\\n    examples = df.apply(lambda row: load_span_example(row, n, nlp), axis=1)\\n\\n    # Add prompts and continuations to dataframe\\n    df = df[examples.notna()]\\n    df['prompt'], df['continuation'] = zip(*examples.dropna())\\n    print(f'Limited to {len(df)} rows after preprocessing')\\n\\n    df = df.head(25_000)\\n\\n    df.to_pickle(out_file)\\n    return df\\n\\n\\nif __name__ == '__main__':\\n    create_prompts_dataset()\\n\"]}]",
      "weight": 0.125,
      "components": []

      "name": "Fine-Tuning on Non-Toxic Data",
      "description": "The pretrained language model is fine-tuned on the selected non-toxic corpus to adapt its behavior. This involves training the model with standard language modeling objectives on the non-toxic data to reduce its propensity for toxic generations.",
      "code": "[{\"file_name\": \"scripts/finetuning/finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py\", \"code_list\": [\"# coding=utf-8\\n# Copyright 2018 The Google AI Language Team Authors and The HuggingFace Inc. team.\\n# Copyright (c) 2018, NVIDIA CORPORATION.  All rights reserved.\\n#\\n# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the \\\"License\\\");\\n# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.\\n# You may obtain a copy of the License at\\n#\\n#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0\\n#\\n# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software\\n# distributed under the License is distributed on an \\\"AS IS\\\" BASIS,\\n# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.\\n# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and\\n# limitations under the License.\\n\\\"\\\"\\\"\\nFine-tuning the library models for language modeling on a text file (GPT, GPT-2, BERT, RoBERTa).\\nGPT and GPT-2 are fine-tuned using a causal language modeling (CLM) loss while BERT and RoBERTa are fine-tuned\\nusing a masked language modeling (MLM) loss.\\n\\\"\\\"\\\"\\n\\nimport logging\\nimport math\\nimport os\\nimport pickle\\nimport time\\nfrom dataclasses import dataclass, field\\nfrom typing import Optional, List\\n\\nimport torch\\nfrom torch.utils.data import Dataset, ConcatDataset\\nfrom tqdm import trange\\nfrom transformers import (\\n    CONFIG_MAPPING,\\n    MODEL_WITH_LM_HEAD_MAPPING,\\n    AutoConfig,\\n    AutoModelWithLMHead,\\n    AutoTokenizer,\\n    DataCollatorForLanguageModeling,\\n    HfArgumentParser,\\n    PreTrainedTokenizer,\\n    Trainer,\\n    TrainingArguments,\\n    set_seed,\\n    torch_distributed_zero_first,\\n)\\n\\nlogger = logging.getLogger(__name__)\\n\\nMODEL_CONFIG_CLASSES = list(MODEL_WITH_LM_HEAD_MAPPING.keys())\\nMODEL_TYPES = tuple(conf.model_type for conf in MODEL_CONFIG_CLASSES)\\n\\n\\nclass CTRLTextDataset(Dataset):\\n    def __init__(\\n            self, tokenizer: PreTrainedTokenizer, file_path: str, block_size: int, ctrl_code_tok: int,\\n            overwrite_cache=False, local_rank=-1,\\n    ):\\n        assert os.path.isfile(file_path)\\n\\n        # Subtract 1 so we can prepend the CTRL code\\n        block_size = block_size - tokenizer.num_special_tokens_to_add(pair=False) - 1\\n\\n        directory, filename = os.path.split(file_path)\\n        cached_features_file = os.path.join(\\n            directory, \\\"cached_lm_{}_{}_{}\\\".format(tokenizer.__class__.__name__, str(block_size), filename, ),\\n        )\\n\\n        with torch_distributed_zero_first(local_rank):\\n            # Make sure only the first process in distributed training processes the dataset,\\n            # and the others will use the cache.\\n\\n            if os.path.exists(cached_features_file) and not overwrite_cache:\\n                start = time.time()\\n                with open(cached_features_file, \\\"rb\\\") as handle:\\n                    self.examples = pickle.load(handle)\\n                logger.info(\\n                    f\\\"Loading features from cached file {cached_features_file} [took %.3f s]\\\", time.time() - start\\n                )\\n\\n            else:\\n                logger.info(f\\\"Creating features from dataset file at {directory}\\\")\\n\\n                self.examples = []\\n                with open(file_path, encoding=\\\"utf-8\\\") as f:\\n                    text = f.read()\\n\\n                tokenized_text = tokenizer.convert_tokens_to_ids(tokenizer.tokenize(text))\\n\\n                for i in trange(0, len(tokenized_text) - block_size + 1, block_size):  # Note that we are losing the last truncated example here for the sake of simplicity (no padding)\\n                # If your dataset is small, first you should loook for a bigger one :-) and second you\\n                # can change this behavior by adding (model specific) padding.\\n\\n                start = time.time()\\n                with open(cached_features_file, \\\"wb\\\") as handle:\\n                    pickle.dump(self.examples, handle, protocol=pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL)\\n                logger.info(\\n                    f\\\"Saving features into cached file %s [took %.3f s]\\\", cached_features_file, time.time() - start\\n                )\\n\\n    def __len__(self):\\n        return len(self.examples)\\n\\n    def __getitem__(self, i) -> torch.Tensor:\\n        return torch.tensor(self.examples[i], dtype=torch.long)\\n\\n\\n@dataclass\\nclass ModelArguments:\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Arguments pertaining to which model/config/tokenizer we are going to fine-tune, or train from scratch.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n\\n    model_name_or_path: Optional[str] = field(\\n        default=None,\\n        metadata={\\n            \\\"help\\\": \\\"The model checkpoint for weights initialization. 2. Overview of the Code repository:
# Real Toxicity Prompts Project Overview

## Overview
This repository contains the code and data for the *Real Toxicity Prompts* project by AllenAI, which investigates the propensity of language models to generate toxic content. The project focuses on analyzing toxicity in generated text from models like GPT-2 and GPT-3 when given prompts from real-world sources. Key components include:
- Toxicity scoring via Perspective API
- Text generation using multiple models (GPT-2, PPLM)
- Dataset processing and analysis tools
- Notebooks for exploratory analysis

The project is associated with a [research paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.11462) and has a [demo website](https://toxicdegeneration.allenai.org/). The data is available separately on the AllenAI website.

## System Architecture

### Main Components
1. **Data Processing** (`scripts/data/`):
   - Tools for processing webtext data (`unpack_openwebtext.py`, `process_urls.py`)
   - Dataset creation utilities (`create_prompts_dataset.py`, `create_text_db.py`)
   - Specialized processing (African American English extraction, duplicate detection)

2. **Model Generation** (`generation/`):
   - Base generation framework (`generation.py`)
   - Specific model implementations (GPT-2, PPLM)
   - Notebooks for analysis of generations

3. **Model Training** (`scripts/finetuning/`):
   - Fine-tuning scripts for affect language models
   - Control codes implementation for GPT-2

4. **Evaluation** (`scripts/evaluation/`):
   - Language modeling evaluation on webtext
   - Custom dataset handling

5. **Utilities** (`utils/`):
   - Database interface (`db.py`)
   - Perspective API wrapper (`perspective_api.py`)
   - General utilities and constants

### Workflow
1. Data preparation (webtext processing → prompt dataset creation)
2. Model fine-tuning (optional)
3. Generation experiments (run via `run_prompts_experiment.py`)
4. Analysis (via provided notebooks)

## Core Features

### Data Features
- **Real-world prompt dataset**: Contains prompts from various sources with toxicity annotations
- **Naughty words list**: Curated list of potentially offensive terms (`data/list_of_naughty_and_bad_words.txt`)
- **Webtext processing**: Tools to handle large-scale text data

### Generation Capabilities
- Multiple generation methods:
  - Standard GPT-2 (`gpt2_generation.py`)
  - PPLM (Plug and Play Language Models) variant (`pplm_generation.py`)
- Control mechanisms for generation parameters

### Toxicity Analysis
- Integration with Perspective API for toxicity scoring
- Notebooks for analyzing generation results (`notebooks/realtoxicityprompts_results.ipynb`)
- Comparative analysis tools

### Model Customization
- Fine-tuning scripts for:
  - Affect language models (`finetune_affect_lm.py`)
  - Controlled GPT-2 generation (`finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py`)

## Usage
The project is designed to be run in a Conda environment (`rtp`) with all dependencies specified in `environment.yml`. Main entry points are:
- `scripts/run_prompts_experiment.py` for running generation experiments
- Jupyter notebooks for analysis
- Individual scripts for specific data processing tasks

## Dependencies
Key external dependencies include:
- Perspective API (for toxicity scoring)
- OpenAI API (for GPT-3 access)
- PyTorch (for model implementations)
- Transformers library (for language models)

The project provides a comprehensive framework for studying toxicity in language model generations, with particular attention to real-world scenarios and controlled experiments.

AutoTokenizer,\\\\n    DataCollatorForLanguageModeling,\\\\n    HfArgumentParser,\\\\n    PreTrainedTokenizer,\\\\n    Trainer,\\\\n    TrainingArguments,\\\\n    set_seed,\\\\n    torch_distributed_zero_first,\\\\n)\\\\n\\\\nlogger = logging.getLogger(__name__)\\\\n\\\\nMODEL_CONFIG_CLASSES = list(MODEL_WITH_LM_HEAD_MAPPING.keys())\\\\nMODEL_TYPES = tuple(conf.model_type for conf in MODEL_CONFIG_CLASSES)\\\\n\\\\n\\\\nclass CTRLTextDataset(Dataset):\\\\n    def __init__(\\\\n            self, tokenizer: PreTrainedTokenizer, file_path: str, block_size: int, ctrl_code_tok: int,\\\\n            overwrite_cache=False, local_rank=-1,\\\\n    ):\\\\n        assert os.path.isfile(file_path)\\\\n\\\\n        # Subtract 1 so we can prepend the CTRL code\\\\n        block_size = block_size - tokenizer.num_special_tokens_to_add(pair=False) - 1\\\\n\\\\n        directory, filename = os.path.split(file_path)\\\\n        cached_features_file = os.path.join(\\\\n            directory, \\\\\\"cached_lm_{}_{}_{}\\\\\\".format(tokenizer.__class__.__name__, str(block_size), filename, ),\\\\n        )\\\\n\\\\n        with torch_distributed_zero_first(local_rank):\\\\n            # Make sure only the first process in distributed training processes the dataset,\\\\n            # and the others will use the cache.\\\\n\\\\n            if os.path.exists(cached_features_file) and not overwrite_cache:\\\\n                start = time.time()\\\\n                with open(cached_features_file, \\\\\\"rb\\\\\\") as handle:\\\\n                    self.examples = pickle.load(handle)\\\\n                logger.info(\\\\n                    f\\\\\\"Loading features from cached file {cached_features_file} [took %.3f s]\\\\\\", time.time() - start\\\\n                )\\\\n\\\\n            else:\\\\n                logger.info(f\\\\\\"Creating features from dataset file at {directory}\\\\\\")\\\\n\\\\n                self.examples = []\\\\n                with open(file_path, encoding=\\\\\\"utf-8\\\\\\") as f:\\\\n                    text = f.read()\\\\n\\\\n                tokenized_text = tokenizer.convert_tokens_to_ids(tokenizer.tokenize(text))\\\\n\\\\n                for i in trange(0, len(tokenized_text) - block_size + 1, block_size):  # Truncate in block of block_size\\\\n                    self.examples.append(\\\\n                        tokenizer.build_inputs_with_special_tokens([ctrl_code_tok] + tokenized_text[i: i + block_size])\\\\n                    )\\\\n                # Note that we are losing the last truncated example here for the sake of simplicity (no padding)\\\\n                # If your dataset is small, first you should loook for a bigger one :-) and second you\\\\n                # can change this behavior by adding (model specific) padding.\\\\n\\\\n                start = time.time()\\\\n                with open(cached_features_file, \\\\\\"wb\\\\\\") as handle:\\\\n                    pickle.dump(self.examples, handle, protocol=pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL)\\\\n                logger.info(\\\\n                    f\\\\\\"Saving features into cached file %s [took %.3f s]\\\\\\", cached_features_file, time.time() - start\\\\n                )\\\\n\\\\n    def __len__(self):\\\\n        return len(self.examples)\\\\n\\\\n    def __getitem__(self, i) -> torch.Tensor:\\\\n        return torch.tensor(self.examples[i], dtype=torch.long)\\\\n\\\\n\\\\n@dataclass\\\\nclass ModelArguments:\\\\n    \\\\\\"\\\\\\"\\\\\\"\\\\n metadata={\\\\\\"help\\\\\\": \\\\\\"Overwrite the cached training and evaluation sets\\\\\\"}\\\\n    )\\\\n\\\\n\\\\ndef get_dataset(args: DataTrainingArguments, tokenizer: PreTrainedTokenizer, ctrl_codes: List[int], evaluate=False,\\\\n                local_rank=-1):\\\\n    file_paths = args.eval_data_files if evaluate else args.train_data_files\\\\n    datasets = []\\\\n    for file_path, This is not supported, but you can do it from another script, save it,\\\\\\"\\\\n            \\\\\\"and load it from here, using --tokenizer_name\\\\\\"\\\\n        )\\\\n\\\\n    # Add CTRL codes to vocab\\\\n    assert len(data_args.ctrl_codes) == len(data_args.eval_data_files) == len(data_args.ctrl_codes)\\\\n    print(\\\\\\"CTRL codes:\\\\\\", *data_args.ctrl_codes)\\\\n    num_added_toks = tokenizer.add_tokens(data_args.ctrl_codes)\\\\n    assert num_added_toks == len(data_args.ctrl_codes)\\\\n    ctrl_code_toks = tokenizer.encode(data_args.ctrl_codes)\\\\n\\\\n    if model_args.model_name_or_path:\\\\n        model = AutoModelWithLMHead.from_pretrained(\\\\n            model_args.model_name_or_path,\\\\n            from_tf=bool(\\\\\\".ckpt\\\\\\" in model_args.model_name_or_path),\\\\n            config=config,\\\\n            cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir,\\\\n        )\\\\n    else:\\\\n        logger.info(\\\\\\"Training new model from scratch\\\\\\")\\\\n        model = AutoModelWithLMHead.from_config(config)\\\\n\\\\n    model.resize_token_embeddings(len(tokenizer))\\\\n\\\\n    if config.model_type in [\\\\\\"bert\\\\\\", \\\\\\"roberta\\\\\\", \\\\\\"distilbert\\\\\\", \\\\\\"camembert\\\\\\"] and not data_args.mlm:\\\\n        raise ValueError(\\\\n            \\\\\\"BERT and RoBERTa-like models do not have LM heads but masked LM heads. Overview of the Code repository:\n# Real Toxicity Prompts Project Overview\n\n## Overview\nThis repository contains the code and data for the *Real Toxicity Prompts* project by AllenAI, which investigates the propensity of language models to generate toxic content. The project focuses on analyzing toxicity in generated text from models like GPT-2 and GPT-3 when given prompts from real-world sources. Key components include:\n- Toxicity scoring via Perspective API\n- Text generation using multiple models (GPT-2, PPLM)\n- Dataset processing and analysis tools\n- Notebooks for exploratory analysis\n\nThe project is associated with a [research paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.11462) and has a [demo website](https://toxicdegeneration.allenai.org/). The data is available separately on the AllenAI website.\n\n## System Architecture\n\n### Main Components\n1. **Data Processing** (`scripts/data/`):\n   - Tools for processing webtext data (`unpack_openwebtext.py`, `process_urls.py`)\n   - Dataset creation utilities (`create_prompts_dataset.py`, `create_text_db.py`)\n   - Specialized processing (African American English extraction, duplicate detection)\n\n2. **Model Generation** (`generation/`):\n   - Base generation framework (`generation.py`)\n   - Specific model implementations (GPT-2, PPLM)\n   - Notebooks for analysis of generations\n\n3. **Model Training** (`scripts/finetuning/`):\n   - Fine-tuning scripts for affect language models\n   - Control codes implementation for GPT-2\n\n4. **Evaluation** (`scripts/evaluation/`):\n   - Language modeling evaluation on webtext\n   - Custom dataset handling\n\n5. **Utilities** (`utils/`):\n   - Database interface (`db.py`)\n   - Perspective API wrapper (`perspective_api.py`)\n   - General utilities and constants\n\n### Workflow\n1. Data preparation (webtext processing → prompt dataset creation)\n2. Model fine-tuning (optional)\n3. Generation experiments (run via `run_prompts_experiment.py`)\n4. Analysis (via provided notebooks)\n\n## Core Features\n\n### Data Features\n- **Real-world prompt dataset**: Contains prompts from various sources with toxicity annotations\n- **Naughty words list**: Curated list of potentially offensive terms (`data/list_of_naughty_and_bad_words.txt`)\n- **Webtext processing**: Tools to handle large-scale text data\n\n### Generation Capabilities\n- Multiple generation methods:\n  - Standard GPT-2 (`gpt2_generation.py`)\n  - PPLM (Plug and Play Language Models) variant (`pplm_generation.py`)\n- Control mechanisms for generation parameters\n\n### Toxicity Analysis\n- Integration with Perspective API for toxicity scoring\n- Notebooks for analyzing generation results (`notebooks/realtoxicityprompts_results.ipynb`)\n- Comparative analysis tools\n\n### Model Customization\n- Fine-tuning scripts for:\n  - Affect language models (`finetune_affect_lm.py`)\n  - Controlled GPT-2 generation (`finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py`)\n\n## Usage\nThe project is designed to be run in a Conda environment (`rtp`) with all dependencies specified in `environment.yml`. Main entry points are:\n- `scripts/run_prompts_experiment.py` for running generation experiments\n- Jupyter notebooks for analysis\n- Individual scripts for specific data processing tasks\n\n## Dependencies\nKey external dependencies include:\n- Perspective API (for toxicity scoring)\n- OpenAI API (for GPT-3 access)\n- PyTorch (for model implementations)\n- Transformers library (for language models)\n\nThe project provides a comprehensive framework for studying toxicity in language model generations, with particular attention to real-world scenarios and controlled experiments.\n\n3. Relevant Code Files:\n[\n  {\n    "file_name": "generation/pplm_generation.py",\n    "code_list": [\n      "           **kwargs):\\n        # Set random seed\\n        torch.manual_seed(seed)\\n        np.random.seed(seed)\\n\\n        pretrained_model = DISCRIMINATOR_MODELS_PARAMS[discrim][\\"pretrained_model\\"]\\n        print(\\"discrim = {}, pretrained_model set \\" \\"to discriminator\'s = {}\\".format(discrim, pretrained_model))\\n\\n        # Default parameters correspond to those in the PPLM paper for the toxicity discriminative model\\n    # Others (such as sampling) taken from https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/tree/master/examples/pplm\\n    def __call__(self,\\n                 cond_text=\'\',\\n                 num_samples=1,\\n                 class_label=-1,\\n                 length=20,\\n                 stepsize=0.02,\\n                 temperature=1.0,\\n      ",\n      "k=top_k, probs=True)  # + SMALL_CONST\\n\\n            # rescale\\n            if torch.sum(pert_probs) <= 1:\\n                pert_probs = pert_probs / torch.sum(pert_probs)\\n\\n        else:\\n            pert_logits = top_k_filter(pert_logits, k=top_k)  # + SMALL_CONST\\n            pert_probs = F.softmax(pert_logits, dim=-1)\\n\\n        # sample or greedy\\n        if sample:\\n            last = torch.multinomial(pert_probs, num_samples=1)\\n\\n        else:\\n            _, last = torch.topk(pert_probs, k=1, dim=-1)\\n\\n        # update context/output_so_far appending the new token\\n        output_so_far = last if output_so_far is None else torch.cat((output_so_far, last), dim=1)\\n\\n        # print(tokenizer.decode(output_so_far.tolist()[0]))\\n\\n    return output_so_far, unpert_discrim_loss, loss_in_time\\n\\n\\nclass PPLMGeneration(Pipeline):\\n    def __init__(self,\\n                 discrim: str,\\n                 seed=0,\\n                 **kwargs):\\n        # In the repository the file that performs the non‐toxic selection is the data creation script (create_prompts_dataset.py) and the file that carries out the fine‐tuning on the selected corpus is the GPT‑2 control fine‐tuning script (finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py).

• scripts/finetuning/finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py  
• scripts/data/create_prompts_dataset.py

2025-09-18 23:32:40,945 - tools.graph_constructor - DEBUG - Technique: Domain-Adaptive Pretraining (DAPT), A detoxification technique where a pretrained language model is further pretrained on a non-toxic subset of a balanced corpus. This method leverages the framework outlined in previous work to adapt the model's behavior towards generating less toxic text. The process involves selecting the bottom 2 percentiles of documents by toxicity from the OpenWebText corpus, ensuring the training data is disjoint from the prompts used for evaluation. The model is then fine-tuned on this non-toxic data to steer its generations away from toxicity.

Subtract 1 so we can prepend the CTRL code\\n        block_size = block_size - tokenizer.num_special_tokens_to_add(pair=False) - 1\\n\\n        directory, filename = os.path.split(file_path)\\n        cached_features_file = os.path.join(\\n            directory, \\\"cached_lm_{}_{}_{}\\\".format(tokenizer.__class__.__name__, str(block_size), filename, ),\\n        )\\n\\n        with torch_distributed_zero_first(local_rank):\\n            # Make sure only the first process in distributed training processes the dataset,\\n            # and the others will use the cache.\\n\\n            if os.path.exists(cached_features_file) and not overwrite_cache:\\n                start = time.time()\\n                with open(cached_features_file, \\\"rb\\\") as handle:\\n                    self.examples = pickle.load(handle)\\n                logger.info(\\n                    f\\\"Loading features from cached file {cached_features_file} [took %.3f s]\\\", time.time() - start\\n                )\\n\\n            else:\\n                logger.info(f\\\"Creating features from dataset file at {directory}\\\")\\n\\n                self.examples = []\\n                with open(file_path, encoding=\\\"utf-8\\\") as f:\\n                    text = f.read()\\n\\n                tokenized_text = tokenizer.convert_tokens_to_ids(tokenizer.tokenize(text))\\n\\n                for i in trange(0, len(tokenized_text) - block_size + 1, block_size):  # Truncate in block of block_size\\n                    self.examples.append(\\n                        tokenizer.build_inputs_with_special_tokens([ctrl_code_tok] + tokenized_text[i: i + block_size])\\n                    )\\n                # Note that we are losing the last truncated example here for the sake of simplicity (no padding)\\n                # If your dataset is small, first you should loook for a bigger one :-) and second you\\n                # can change this behavior by adding (model specific) padding.\\n\\n                start = time.time()\\n                with open(cached_features_file, \\\"wb\\\") as handle:\\n                    pickle.dump(self.examples, handle, protocol=pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL)\\n                logger.info(\\n                    f\\\"Saving features into cached file %s [took %.3f s]\\\", cached_features_file, time.time() - start\\n                )\\n\\n    def __len__(self):\\n        return len(self.examples)\\n\\n    def __getitem__(self, i) -> torch.Tensor:\\n        return torch.tensor(self.examples[i], dtype=torch.long)\\n\\n\\n@dataclass\\nclass ModelArguments:\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    Arguments pertaining to which model/config/tokenizer we are going to fine-tune, or train from scratch.\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n\\n    model_name_or_path: Optional[str] = field(\\n        default=None,\\n        metadata={\\n            \\\"help\\\": \\\"The model checkpoint for weights initialization. 2. Overview of the Code repository:
# Real Toxicity Prompts Project Overview

## Overview
This repository contains the code and data for the *Real Toxicity Prompts* project by AllenAI, which investigates the propensity of language models to generate toxic content. The project focuses on analyzing toxicity in generated text from models like GPT-2 and GPT-3 when given prompts from real-world sources. Key components include:
- Toxicity scoring via Perspective API
- Text generation using multiple models (GPT-2, PPLM)
- Dataset processing and analysis tools
- Notebooks for exploratory analysis

The project is associated with a [research paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.11462) and has a [demo website](https://toxicdegeneration.allenai.org/). The data is available separately on the AllenAI website.

## System Architecture

### Main Components
1. **Data Processing** (`scripts/data/`):
   - Tools for processing webtext data (`unpack_openwebtext.py`, `process_urls.py`)
   - Dataset creation utilities (`create_prompts_dataset.py`, `create_text_db.py`)
   - Specialized processing (African American English extraction, duplicate detection)

2. **Model Generation** (`generation/`):
   - Base generation framework (`generation.py`)
   - Specific model implementations (GPT-2, PPLM)
   - Notebooks for analysis of generations

3. **Model Training** (`scripts/finetuning/`):
   - Fine-tuning scripts for affect language models
   - Control codes implementation for GPT-2

4. **Evaluation** (`scripts/evaluation/`):
   - Language modeling evaluation on webtext
   - Custom dataset handling

5. **Utilities** (`utils/`):
   - Database interface (`db.py`)
   - Perspective API wrapper (`perspective_api.py`)
   - General utilities and constants

### Workflow
1. Data preparation (webtext processing → prompt dataset creation)
2. Model fine-tuning (optional)
3. Generation experiments (run via `run_prompts_experiment.py`)
4. Analysis (via provided notebooks)

## Core Features

### Data Features
- **Real-world prompt dataset**: Contains prompts from various sources with toxicity annotations
- **Naughty words list**: Curated list of potentially offensive terms (`data/list_of_naughty_and_bad_words.txt`)
- **Webtext processing**: Tools to handle large-scale text data

### Generation Capabilities
- Multiple generation methods:
  - Standard GPT-2 (`gpt2_generation.py`)
  - PPLM (Plug and Play Language Models) variant (`pplm_generation.py`)
- Control mechanisms for generation parameters

### Toxicity Analysis
- Integration with Perspective API for toxicity scoring
- Notebooks for analyzing generation results (`notebooks/realtoxicityprompts_results.ipynb`)
- Comparative analysis tools

### Model Customization
- Fine-tuning scripts for:
  - Affect language models (`finetune_affect_lm.py`)
  - Controlled GPT-2 generation (`finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py`)

## Usage
The project is designed to be run in a Conda environment (`rtp`) with all dependencies specified in `environment.yml`. Main entry points are:
- `scripts/run_prompts_experiment.py` for running generation experiments
- Jupyter notebooks for analysis
- Individual scripts for specific data processing tasks

## Dependencies
Key external dependencies include:
- Perspective API (for toxicity scoring)
- OpenAI API (for GPT-3 access)
- PyTorch (for model implementations)
- Transformers library (for language models)

The project provides a comprehensive framework for studying toxicity in language model generations, with particular attention to real-world scenarios and controlled experiments.

3. Relevant Code Files:

      "from typing import Optional\n\nimport torch\nfrom torch import nn\nfrom torch.nn import CrossEntropyLoss\nfrom transformers import GPT2LMHeadModel\n\nNUM_AFFECTS = 2\n\n\nclass Affect(nn.Module):\n    \"\"\"\n    See section 4.3 of Ghosh:\n        Affect-LM can also be used as a language model where the next predicted word is\n        estimated from the words in the context, along with an affect category extracted\n        from the context words themselves (instead of being encoded externally as in generation).\n\n    beta in [1.0, 1.5, 1.75, 2.0, 2.25, 2.5, 3.0]\n    \"\"\"\n\n    IGNORE_IDS = [50256, 247, 251, 250]\n\n    def __init__(self,\n                 affect_dim: int,\n                 vocab_size: int,\n                 beta: float = 1.0,\n                 ignore_special_tokens=False):\n        super().__init__()\n        self.beta = beta\n        self.ignore_special_tokens = ignore_special_tokens\n        self.affect2vocab = nn.Linear(affect_dim, vocab_size, bias=True)\n        self.init_weights()\n\n    def forward(self, affect_labels: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:\n        out = self.affect2vocab(affect_labels)\n        if self.ignore_special_tokens:\n            out[:, :, self.IGNORE_IDS] = 0\n        return self.beta * out\n\n    def init_weights(self, initializer_range=0.02):\n        # initializer_range taken from GPT2Config\n        self.affect2vocab.weight.data.normal_(mean=0.0, std=initializer_range)\n        self.affect2vocab.bias.data.zero_()\n\n\nclass AffectGPT2LMHeadModel(GPT2LMHeadModel):\n    affect_labels: Optional[torch.Tensor]\n\n    def __init__(self, config):\n       "
    ]
