training_args.fp16,\\\\n    )\\\\n    logger.info(\\\\\\"Training/evaluation parameters %s\\\\\\", training_args)\\\\n\\\\n    # Set seed\\\\n    set_seed(training_args.seed)\\\\n\\\\n    # Load pretrained model and tokenizer\\\\n    #\\\\n    # Distributed training:\\\\n    # The .from_pretrained methods guarantee that only one local process can concurrently\\\\n    # download model & vocab.\\\\n\\\\n    if model_args.config_name:\\\\n        config = AutoConfig.from_pretrained(model_args.config_name, cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir)\\\\n    elif model_args.model_name_or_path:\\\\n        config = AutoConfig.from_pretrained(model_args.model_name_or_path, cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir)\\\\n    else:\\\\n        config = CONFIG_MAPPING[model_args.model_type]()\\\\n        logger.warning(\\\\\\"You are instantiating a new config instance from scratch.\\\\\\")\\\\n\\\\n    if model_args.tokenizer_name:\\\\n        tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_args.tokenizer_name, cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir)\\\\n    elif model_args.model_name_or_path:\\\\n        tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_args.model_name_or_path, cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir)\\\\n    else:\\\\n        raise ValueError(\\\\n            \\\\\\"You are instantiating a new tokenizer from scratch. This is not supported, but you can do it from another script, save it,\\\\\\"\\\\n            \\\\\\"and load it from here, using --tokenizer_name\\\\\\"\\\\n        )\\\\n\\\\n    inline_meta = None\\\\n    if model_args.controllable_model == \'ctrl-gpt2\':\\\\n        print(\\\\\\"Loaded added tokens:\\\\\\", tokenizer.added_tokens_encoder)\\\\n        inline_meta = TARGET_CTRL_CODE\\\\n        assert inline_meta in tokenizer.get_vocab().keys()\\\\n        print(\\\\\\"Using inline meta:\\\\\\", inline_meta)\\\\n\\\\n    if model_args.model_name_or_path:\\\\n        controllable_model = model_args.controllable_model\\\\n        if controllable_model and controllable_model.startswith(\'affect-gpt2\'):\\\\n            logger.info(\\\\\\"Using AffectLM for evaluation\\\\\\")\\\\n            model = AffectGPT2LMHeadModel.from_pretrained(\\\\n                model_args.model_name_or_path,\\\\n                from_tf=bool(\\\\\\".ckpt\\\\\\" in model_args.model_name_or_path),\\\\n                config=config,\\\\n                cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir,\\\\n            )\\\\n\\\\n            # This conditions the model to generate text aligned with the specified toxicity level.",\n      "code": "[{\\"file_name\\": \\"scripts/finetuning/finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py\\", \\"code_list\\": [\\"# coding=utf-8\\\\n# Copyright 2018 The Google AI Language Team Authors and The HuggingFace Inc. team.\\\\n# Copyright (c) 2018, NVIDIA CORPORATION. using a causal language modeling (CLM) loss while BERT and RoBERTa are fine-tuned\\\\nusing a masked language modeling (MLM) loss.\\\\n\\\\\\"\\\\\\"\\\\\\"\\\\n\\\\nimport logging\\\\nimport math\\\\nimport os\\\\nimport pickle\\\\nimport time\\\\nfrom dataclasses import dataclass, field\\\\nfrom typing import Optional, List\\\\n\\\\nimport torch\\\\nfrom torch.utils.data import Dataset, ConcatDataset\\\\nfrom tqdm import trange\\\\nfrom transformers import (\\\\n    CONFIG_MAPPING,\\\\n    MODEL_WITH_LM_HEAD_MAPPING,\\\\n    AutoConfig,\\\\n    AutoModelWithLMHead,\\\\n    AutoTokenizer,\\\\n    DataCollatorForLanguageModeling,\\\\n    HfArgumentParser,\\\\n    PreTrainedTokenizer,\\\\n    Trainer,\\\\n    TrainingArguments,\\\\n    set_seed,\\\\n    torch_distributed_zero_first,\\\\n)\\\\n\\\\nlogger = logging.getLogger(__name__)\\\\n\\\\nMODEL_CONFIG_CLASSES = list(MODEL_WITH_LM_HEAD_MAPPING.keys())\\\\nMODEL_TYPES = tuple(conf.model_type for conf in MODEL_CONFIG_CLASSES)\\\\n\\\\n\\\\nclass CTRLTextDataset(Dataset):\\\\n    def __init__(\\\\n            self, tokenizer: PreTrainedTokenizer, file_path: str, block_size: int, ctrl_code_tok: int,\\\\n            overwrite_cache=False, local_rank=-1,\\\\n    ):\\\\n        assert os.path.isfile(file_path)\\\\n\\\\n        # Subtract 1 so we can prepend the CTRL code\\\\n        block_size = block_size - tokenizer.num_special_tokens_to_add(pair=False) - 1\\\\n\\\\n        directory, filename = os.path.split(file_path)\\\\n        cached_features_file = os.path.join(\\\\n            directory, \\\\\\"cached_lm_{}_{}_{}\\\\\\".format(tokenizer.__class__.__name__, str(block_size), filename, ),\\\\n        )\\\\n\\\\n        with torch_distributed_zero_first(local_rank):\\\\n            # Make sure only the first process in distributed training processes the dataset,\\\\n            # and the others will use the cache.\\\\n\\\\n            if os.path.exists(cached_features_file) and not overwrite_cache:\\\\n                start = time.time()\\\\n                with open(cached_features_file, \\\\\\"rb\\\\\\") as handle:\\\\n                    self.examples = pickle.load(handle)\\\\n                logger.info(\\\\n                    f\\\\\\"Loading features from cached file {cached_features_file} [took %.3f s]\\\\\\", time.time() - start\\\\n                )\\\\n\\\\n            else:\\\\n                logger.info(f\\\\\\"Creating features from dataset file at {directory}\\\\\\")\\\\n\\\\n                self.examples = []\\\\n                with open(file_path, encoding=\\\\\\"utf-8\\\\\\") as f:\\\\n                    text = f.read()\\\\n\\\\n                tokenized_text = tokenizer.convert_tokens_to_ids(tokenizer.tokenize(text))\\\\n\\\\n                for i in trange(0, len(tokenized_text) - block_size + 1, block_size):  # Truncate in block of block_size\\\\n                    self.examples.append(\\\\n                        tokenizer.build_inputs_with_special_tokens([ctrl_code_tok] + tokenized_text[i: i + block_size])\\\\n                    )\\\\n                # Note that we are losing the last truncated example here for the sake of simplicity (no padding)\\\\n                # If your dataset is small, first you should loook for a bigger one :-) and second you\\\\n                # can change this behavior by adding (model specific) padding.\\\\n\\\\n                start = time.time()\\\\n                with open(cached_features_file, \\\\\\"wb\\\\\\") as handle:\\\\n                    pickle.dump(self.examples, handle, protocol=pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL)\\\\n                logger.info(\\\\n                    f\\\\\\"Saving features into cached file %s [took %.3f s]\\\\\\", cached_features_file, time.time() - start\\\\n                )\\\\n\\\\n    def __len__(self):\\\\n        return len(self.examples)\\\\n\\\\n    def __getitem__(self, i) -> torch.Tensor:\\\\n        return torch.tensor(self.examples[i], dtype=torch.long)\\\\n\\\\n\\\\n@dataclass\\\\nclass ModelArguments:\\\\n    \\\\\\"\\\\\\"\\\\\\"\\\\n    Arguments pertaining to which model/config/tokenizer we are going to fine-tune, or train from scratch.\\\\n    \\\\\\"\\\\\\"\\\\\\"\\\\n\\\\n    model_name_or_path: Optional[str] = field(\\\\n ctrl_codes: List[int], evaluate=False,\\\\n                local_rank=-1):\\\\n    file_paths = args.eval_data_files if evaluate else args.train_data_files\\\\n    datasets = []\\\\n    for file_path, Use --overwrite_output_dir to overcome.\\\\\\"\\\\n        )\\\\n\\\\n    # Setup logging\\\\n    logging.basicConfig(\\\\n        format=\\\\\\"%(asctime)s - %(levelname)s - %(name)s -   %(message)s\\\\\\",\\\\n        datefmt=\\\\\\"%m/%d/%Y %H:%M:%S\\\\\\",\\\\n        level=logging.INFO if training_args.local_rank in [-1, 0] else logging.WARN,\\\\n    )\\\\n    logger.warning(\\\\n        \\\\\\"Process rank: %s, device: %s, n_gpu: %s, distributed training: %s, 16-bits training: %s\\\\\\",\\\\n        training_args.local_rank,\\\\n        training_args.device,\\\\n        training_args.n_gpu,\\\\n        bool(training_args.local_rank != -1),\\\\n        training_args.fp16,\\\\n    )\\\\n    logger.info(\\\\\\"Training/evaluation parameters %s\\\\\\", training_args)\\\\n\\\\n    # Set seed\\\\n    set_seed(training_args.seed)\\\\n\\\\n    # Load pretrained model and tokenizer\\\\n    #\\\\n    # Distributed training:\\\\n    # The .from_pretrained methods guarantee that only one local process can concurrently\\\\n    # download model & vocab.\\\\n\\\\n    if model_args.config_name:\\\\n        config = AutoConfig.from_pretrained(model_args.config_name, cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir)\\\\n    elif model_args.model_name_or_path:\\\\n        config = AutoConfig.from_pretrained(model_args.model_name_or_path, cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir)\\\\n    else:\\\\n        config = CONFIG_MAPPING[model_args.model_type]()\\\\n        logger.warning(\\\\\\"You are instantiating a new config instance from scratch.\\\\\\")\\\\n\\\\n    if model_args.tokenizer_name:\\\\n        tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_args.tokenizer_name, cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir)\\\\n    elif model_args.model_name_or_path:\\\\n        tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_args.model_name_or_path, cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir)\\\\n    else:\\\\n        raise ValueError(\\\\n            \\\\\\"You are instantiating a new tokenizer from scratch. This is not supported, but you can do it from another script, save it,\\\\\\"\\\\n            \\\\\\"and load it from here, using --tokenizer_name\\\\\\"\\\\n        )\\\\n\\\\n    # Add CTRL codes to vocab\\\\n    assert len(data_args.ctrl_codes) == len(data_args.eval_data_files) == len(data_args.ctrl_codes)\\\\n    print(\\\\\\"CTRL codes:\\\\\\", *data_args.ctrl_codes)\\\\n    num_added_toks = tokenizer.add_tokens(data_args.ctrl_codes)\\\\n    assert num_added_toks == len(data_args.ctrl_codes)\\\\n    ctrl_code_toks = tokenizer.encode(data_args.ctrl_codes)\\\\n\\\\n    if model_args.model_name_or_path:\\\\n        model = AutoModelWithLMHead.from_pretrained(\\\\n            model_args.model_name_or_path,\\\\n            from_tf=bool(\\\\\\".ckpt\\\\\\" in model_args.model_name_or_path),\\\\n            config=config,\\\\n            cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir,\\\\n        )\\\\n    else:\\\\n        logger.info(\\\\\\"Training new model from scratch\\\\\\")\\\\n        model = AutoModelWithLMHead.from_config(config)\\\\n\\\\n    model.resize_token_embeddings(len(tokenizer))\\\\n\\\\n    if config.model_type in [\\\\\\"bert\\\\\\", \\\\\\"roberta\\\\\\", \\\\\\"distilbert\\\\\\", \\\\\\"camembert\\\\\\"] and not data_args.mlm:\\\\n        raise ValueError(\\\\n            \\\\\\"BERT and RoBERTa-like models do not have LM heads but masked LM heads. Overview of the Code repository:\n# Real Toxicity Prompts Project Overview\n\n## Overview\nThis repository contains the code and data for the *Real Toxicity Prompts* project by AllenAI, which investigates the propensity of language models to generate toxic content. The project focuses on analyzing toxicity in generated text from models like GPT-2 and GPT-3 when given prompts from real-world sources. Key components include:\n- Toxicity scoring via Perspective API\n- Text generation using multiple models (GPT-2, PPLM)\n- Dataset processing and analysis tools\n- Notebooks for exploratory analysis\n\nThe project is associated with a [research paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.11462) and has a [demo website](https://toxicdegeneration.allenai.org/). The data is available separately on the AllenAI website.\n\n## System Architecture\n\n### Main Components\n1. **Data Processing** (`scripts/data/`):\n   - Tools for processing webtext data (`unpack_openwebtext.py`, `process_urls.py`)\n   - Dataset creation utilities (`create_prompts_dataset.py`, `create_text_db.py`)\n   - Specialized processing (African American English extraction, duplicate detection)\n\n2. **Model Generation** (`generation/`):\n   - Base generation framework (`generation.py`)\n   - Specific model implementations (GPT-2, PPLM)\n   - Notebooks for analysis of generations\n\n3. **Model Training** (`scripts/finetuning/`):\n   - Fine-tuning scripts for affect language models\n   - Control codes implementation for GPT-2\n\n4. **Evaluation** (`scripts/evaluation/`):\n   - Language modeling evaluation on webtext\n   - Custom dataset handling\n\n5. **Utilities** (`utils/`):\n   - Database interface (`db.py`)\n   - Perspective API wrapper (`perspective_api.py`)\n   - General utilities and constants\n\n### Workflow\n1. Data preparation (webtext processing → prompt dataset creation)\n2. Model fine-tuning (optional)\n3. Generation experiments (run via `run_prompts_experiment.py`)\n4. Analysis (via provided notebooks)\n\n## Core Features\n\n### Data Features\n- **Real-world prompt dataset**: Contains prompts from various sources with toxicity annotations\n- **Naughty words list**: Curated list of potentially offensive terms (`data/list_of_naughty_and_bad_words.txt`)\n- **Webtext processing**: Tools to handle large-scale text data\n\n### Generation Capabilities\n- Multiple generation methods:\n  - Standard GPT-2 (`gpt2_generation.py`)\n  - PPLM (Plug and Play Language Models) variant (`pplm_generation.py`)\n- Control mechanisms for generation parameters\n\n### Toxicity Analysis\n- Integration with Perspective API for toxicity scoring\n- Notebooks for analyzing generation results (`notebooks/realtoxicityprompts_results.ipynb`)\n- Comparative analysis tools\n\n### Model Customization\n- Fine-tuning scripts for:\n  - Affect language models (`finetune_affect_lm.py`)\n  - Controlled GPT-2 generation (`finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py`)\n\n## Usage\nThe project is designed to be run in a Conda environment (`rtp`) with all dependencies specified in `environment.yml`. Main entry points are:\n- `scripts/run_prompts_experiment.py` for running generation experiments\n- Jupyter notebooks for analysis\n- Individual scripts for specific data processing tasks\n\n## Dependencies\nKey external dependencies include:\n- Perspective API (for toxicity scoring)\n- OpenAI API (for GPT-3 access)\n- PyTorch (for model implementations)\n- Transformers library (for language models)\n\nThe project provides a comprehensive framework for studying toxicity in language model generations, with particular attention to real-world scenarios and controlled experiments.\n\n3. • The fine‐tuning script that actually adds the control tokens to the vocabulary and builds datasets where each example is prepended with the toxicity attribute token:
  ─ scripts/finetuning/finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py

• The evaluation and generation setup that uses the added tokens (e.g. setting inline_meta to the target token and gating generation via a “ctrl-gpt2” branch) is also directly relevant:
  ─ scripts/evaluation/run_language_modeling_webtext.py

In addition, the run_prompts_experiment.py file contains a branch for model_type 'gpt2-ctrl' (and so dispatches generation using the control token) that directly applies the technique during generation.

Thus, in descending order of relevance (with the core pretraining implementation coming first):

1. scripts/finetuning/finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py  
2. scripts/evaluation/run_language_modeling_webtext.py  
3. scripts/run_prompts_experiment.py

2025-09-18 23:33:02,803 - tools.graph_constructor - DEBUG - Technique: Attribute Conditioning (CTRLGPT2), A detoxification technique inspired by CTRL, where toxicity attribute tokens (e.g., <|toxic|>, <|nontoxic|>) are prepended to documents during pretraining. The model is conditioned on these tokens to steer its generations towards non-toxic text. The method involves adding these tokens to the vocabulary and continuing pretraining on a randomly sampled corpus with the appropriate attribute tokens prepended. During generation, the <|nontoxic|> token is prepended to prompts to condition the model on non-toxic text.

This representation is used to boost the likelihood of non-toxic tokens during generation. The method involves training a projection layer on a balanced subsample of non-toxic and toxic corpora, then using this layer to adjust the model's vocabulary logits during generation by adding a term that encodes the desired toxicity level.'

      "code": "[{\"file_name\": \"models/affect_lm.py\", \"code_list\": [\"from typing import Optional\\n\\nimport torch\\nfrom torch import nn\\nfrom torch.nn import CrossEntropyLoss\\nfrom transformers import GPT2LMHeadModel\\n\\nNUM_AFFECTS = 2\\n\\n\\nclass Affect(nn.Module):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    See section 4.3 of Ghosh:\\n        Affect-LM can also be used as a language model where the next predicted word is\\n        estimated from the words in the context, along with an affect category extracted\\n        from the context words themselves (instead of being encoded externally as in generation).\\n\\n    beta in [1.0, 1.5, 1.75, 2.0, 2.25, 2.5, 3.0]\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n\\n    IGNORE_IDS = [50256, 247, 251, 250]\\n\\n    def __init__(self,\\n                 affect_dim: int,\\n                 vocab_size: int,\\n                 beta: float = 1.0,\\n                 ignore_special_tokens=False):\\n        super().__init__()\\n        self.beta = beta\\n        self.ignore_special_tokens = ignore_special_tokens\\n        self.affect2vocab = nn.Linear(affect_dim, vocab_size, bias=True)\\n        self.init_weights()\\n\\n    def forward(self, affect_labels: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:\\n        out = self.affect2vocab(affect_labels)\\n        if self.ignore_special_tokens:\\n            out[:, :, self.IGNORE_IDS] = 0\\n        return self.beta * out\\n\\n    def init_weights(self, initializer_range=0.02):\\n        # initializer_range taken from GPT2Config\\n        self.affect2vocab.weight.data.normal_(mean=0.0, std=initializer_range)\\n        self.affect2vocab.bias.data.zero_()\\n\\n\\nclass AffectGPT2LMHeadModel(GPT2LMHeadModel):\\n    affect_labels: Optional[torch.Tensor]\\n\\n    def __init__(self, config):\\n        super().__init__(config)\\n        self.affect = Affect(NUM_AFFECTS, config.vocab_size)\\n        self.affect_labels = None\\n\\n    def freeze_transformer(self):\\n        for p in self.transformer.parameters():\\n            p.requires_grad = False\\n\\n    def freeze_lm_head(self):\\n        for p in self.lm_head.parameters():\\n            p.requires_grad = False\\n\\n    def set_affect_labels(self, affect_labels: torch.Tensor):\\n        print(\\\"Using static affect labels:\\\", affect_labels)\\n        self.affect_labels = affect_labels\\n\\n    def forward(\\n            self,\\n            input_ids=None,\\n            past=None,\\n            attention_mask=None,\\n            token_type_ids=None,\\n            position_ids=None,\\n            head_mask=None,\\n            inputs_embeds=None,\\n            labels=None,\\n            affect_labels=None,\\n    ):\\n        transformer_outputs = self.transformer(\\n            input_ids,\\n            past=past,\\n            attention_mask=attention_mask,\\n            token_type_ids=token_type_ids,\\n            position_ids=position_ids,\\n            head_mask=head_mask,\\n            inputs_embeds=inputs_embeds,\\n        )\\n        hidden_states = transformer_outputs[0]\\n\\n        lm_logits = self.lm_head(hidden_states)\\n\\n        if affect_labels is None and self.affect_labels is not None:\\n            affect_labels = self.affect_labels.repeat(len(input_ids), 1, 1)\\n\\n        if affect_labels is not None and self.affect is not None:\\n            # Add affect logits to lm logits\\n            affect_logits = self.affect(affect_labels)\\n            lm_logits += affect_logits\\n\\n        outputs = (lm_logits,) + transformer_outputs[1:]\\n        if labels is not None:\\n            #       "code": "[{\"file_name\": \"models/affect_lm.py\", \"code_list\": [\"from typing import Optional\\n\\nimport torch\\nfrom torch import nn\\nfrom torch.nn import CrossEntropyLoss\\nfrom transformers import GPT2LMHeadModel\\n\\nNUM_AFFECTS = 2\\n\\n\\nclass Affect(nn.Module):\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n    See section 4.3 of Ghosh:\\n        Affect-LM can also be used as a language model where the next predicted word is\\n        estimated from the words in the context, along with an affect category extracted\\n        from the context words themselves (instead of being encoded externally as in generation).\\n\\n    beta in [1.0, 1.5, 1.75, 2.0, 2.25, 2.5, 3.0]\\n    \\\"\\\"\\\"\\n\\n    IGNORE_IDS = [50256, 247, 251, 250]\\n\\n    def __init__(self,\\n                 affect_dim: int,\\n                 vocab_size: int,\\n                 beta: float = 1.0,\\n                 ignore_special_tokens=False):\\n        super().__init__()\\n        self.beta = beta\\n        self.ignore_special_tokens = ignore_special_tokens\\n        self.affect2vocab = nn.Linear(affect_dim, vocab_size, bias=True)\\n        self.init_weights()\\n\\n    def forward(self, affect_labels: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:\\n        out = self.affect2vocab(affect_labels)\\n        if self.ignore_special_tokens:\\n            out[:, :, self.IGNORE_IDS] = 0\\n        return self.beta * out\\n\\n    def init_weights(self, initializer_range=0.02):\\n        # initializer_range taken from GPT2Config\\n        self.affect2vocab.weight.data.normal_(mean=0.0, std=initializer_range)\\n        self.affect2vocab.bias.data.zero_()\\n\\n\\nclass AffectGPT2LMHeadModel(GPT2LMHeadModel):\\n    affect_labels: Optional[torch.Tensor]\\n\\n    def __init__(self, config):\\n        super().__init__(config)\\n        self.affect = Affect(NUM_AFFECTS, config.vocab_size)\\n        self.affect_labels = None\\n\\n    def freeze_transformer(self):\\n        for p in self.transformer.parameters():\\n            p.requires_grad = False\\n\\n    def freeze_lm_head(self):\\n        for p in self.lm_head.parameters():\\n            p.requires_grad = False\\n\\n    def set_affect_labels(self, affect_labels: torch.Tensor):\\n        print(\\\"Using static affect labels:\\\", affect_labels)\\n        self.affect_labels = affect_labels\\n\\n    def forward(\\n            self,\\n            input_ids=None,\\n            past=None,\\n            attention_mask=None,\\n            token_type_ids=None,\\n            position_ids=None,\\n            head_mask=None,\\n            inputs_embeds=None,\\n            labels=None,\\n            affect_labels=None,\\n    ):\\n        transformer_outputs = self.transformer(\\n            input_ids,\\n            past=past,\\n            attention_mask=attention_mask,\\n            token_type_ids=token_type_ids,\\n            position_ids=position_ids,\\n            head_mask=head_mask,\\n            inputs_embeds=inputs_embeds,\\n        )\\n        hidden_states = transformer_outputs[0]\\n\\n        lm_logits = self.lm_head(hidden_states)\\n\\n        if affect_labels is None and self.affect_labels is not None:\\n            affect_labels = self.affect_labels.repeat(len(input_ids), 1, 1)\\n\\n        if affect_labels is not None and self.affect is not None:\\n            # Add affect logits to lm logits\\n            affect_logits = self.affect(affect_labels)\\n            lm_logits += affect_logits\\n\\n        outputs = (lm_logits,) + transformer_outputs[1:]\\n        if labels is not None:\\n            # 2. Overview of the Code repository:
# Real Toxicity Prompts Project Overview

## Overview
This repository contains the code and data for the *Real Toxicity Prompts* project by AllenAI, which investigates the propensity of language models to generate toxic content. The project focuses on analyzing toxicity in generated text from models like GPT-2 and GPT-3 when given prompts from real-world sources. Key components include:
- Toxicity scoring via Perspective API
- Text generation using multiple models (GPT-2, PPLM)
- Dataset processing and analysis tools
- Notebooks for exploratory analysis

The project is associated with a [research paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.11462) and has a [demo website](https://toxicdegeneration.allenai.org/). The data is available separately on the AllenAI website.

## System Architecture

### Main Components
1. **Data Processing** (`scripts/data/`):
   - Tools for processing webtext data (`unpack_openwebtext.py`, `process_urls.py`)
   - Dataset creation utilities (`create_prompts_dataset.py`, `create_text_db.py`)
   - Specialized processing (African American English extraction, duplicate detection)

2. **Model Generation** (`generation/`):
   - Base generation framework (`generation.py`)
   - Specific model implementations (GPT-2, PPLM)
   - Notebooks for analysis of generations

3. **Model Training** (`scripts/finetuning/`):
   - Fine-tuning scripts for affect language models
   - Control codes implementation for GPT-2

4. **Evaluation** (`scripts/evaluation/`):
   - Language modeling evaluation on webtext
   - Custom dataset handling

5. **Utilities** (`utils/`):
   - Database interface (`db.py`)
   - Perspective API wrapper (`perspective_api.py`)
   - General utilities and constants

### Workflow
1. Data preparation (webtext processing → prompt dataset creation)
2. Model fine-tuning (optional)
3. Generation experiments (run via `run_prompts_experiment.py`)
4. Analysis (via provided notebooks)

## Core Features

### Data Features
- **Real-world prompt dataset**: Contains prompts from various sources with toxicity annotations
- **Naughty words list**: Curated list of potentially offensive terms (`data/list_of_naughty_and_bad_words.txt`)
- **Webtext processing**: Tools to handle large-scale text data

### Generation Capabilities
- Multiple generation methods:
  - Standard GPT-2 (`gpt2_generation.py`)
  - PPLM (Plug and Play Language Models) variant (`pplm_generation.py`)
- Control mechanisms for generation parameters

### Toxicity Analysis
- Integration with Perspective API for toxicity scoring
- Notebooks for analyzing generation results (`notebooks/realtoxicityprompts_results.ipynb`)
- Comparative analysis tools

### Model Customization
- Fine-tuning scripts for:
  - Affect language models (`finetune_affect_lm.py`)
  - Controlled GPT-2 generation (`finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py`)

## Usage
The project is designed to be run in a Conda environment (`rtp`) with all dependencies specified in `environment.yml`. Main entry points are:
- `scripts/run_prompts_experiment.py` for running generation experiments
- Jupyter notebooks for analysis
- Individual scripts for specific data processing tasks

## Dependencies
Key external dependencies include:
- Perspective API (for toxicity scoring)
- OpenAI API (for GPT-3 access)
- PyTorch (for model implementations)
- Transformers library (for language models)

The project provides a comprehensive framework for studying toxicity in language model generations, with particular attention to real-world scenarios and controlled experiments.

      "k=top_k, probs=True)  # + SMALL_CONST\n\n            # rescale\n            if torch.sum(pert_probs) <= 1:\n                pert_probs = pert_probs / torch.sum(pert_probs)\n\n        else:\n            pert_logits = top_k_filter(pert_logits, k=top_k)  # + SMALL_CONST\n            pert_probs = F.softmax(pert_logits, dim=-1)\n\n        # sample or greedy\n        if sample:\n            last = torch.multinomial(pert_probs, num_samples=1)\n\n        else:\n            _, last = torch.topk(pert_probs, k=1, dim=-1)\n\n        # update context/output_so_far appending the new token\n        output_so_far = last if output_so_far is None else torch.cat((output_so_far, last), dim=1)\n\n        # print(tokenizer.decode(output_so_far.tolist()[0]))\n\n    return output_so_far, unpert_discrim_loss, loss_in_time\n\n\nclass PPLMGeneration(Pipeline):\n    def __init__(self,\n                 discrim: str,\n                 seed=0,\n                 **kwargs):\n        # Set random seed\n        torch.manual_seed(seed)\n        np.random.seed(seed)\n\n        pretrained_model = DISCRIMINATOR_MODELS_PARAMS[discrim][\"pretrained_model\"]\n        print(\"discrim = {}, pretrained_model set \" \"to discriminator's = {}\".format(discrim, pretrained_model))\n\n        # load pretrained model\n        model = GPT2LMHeadModel.from_pretrained(pretrained_model, output_hidden_states=True)\n        model.eval()\n\n    ",

Overview of the Code repository:\n# Real Toxicity Prompts Project Overview\n\n## Overview\nThis repository contains the code and data for the *Real Toxicity Prompts* project by AllenAI, which investigates the propensity of language models to generate toxic content. The project focuses on analyzing toxicity in generated text from models like GPT-2 and GPT-3 when given prompts from real-world sources. Key components include:\n- Toxicity scoring via Perspective API\n- Text generation using multiple models (GPT-2, PPLM)\n- Dataset processing and analysis tools\n- Notebooks for exploratory analysis\n\nThe project is associated with a [research paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.11462) and has a [demo website](https://toxicdegeneration.allenai.org/). The data is available separately on the AllenAI website.\n\n## System Architecture\n\n### Main Components\n1. **Data Processing** (`scripts/data/`):\n   - Tools for processing webtext data (`unpack_openwebtext.py`, `process_urls.py`)\n   - Dataset creation utilities (`create_prompts_dataset.py`, `create_text_db.py`)\n   - Specialized processing (African American English extraction, duplicate detection)\n\n2. **Model Generation** (`generation/`):\n   - Base generation framework (`generation.py`)\n   - Specific model implementations (GPT-2, PPLM)\n   - Notebooks for analysis of generations\n\n3. **Model Training** (`scripts/finetuning/`):\n   - Fine-tuning scripts for affect language models\n   - Control codes implementation for GPT-2\n\n4. **Evaluation** (`scripts/evaluation/`):\n   - Language modeling evaluation on webtext\n   - Custom dataset handling\n\n5. **Utilities** (`utils/`):\n   - Database interface (`db.py`)\n   - Perspective API wrapper (`perspective_api.py`)\n   - General utilities and constants\n\n### Workflow\n1. Data preparation (webtext processing → prompt dataset creation)\n2. Model fine-tuning (optional)\n3. Generation experiments (run via `run_prompts_experiment.py`)\n4. Analysis (via provided notebooks)\n\n## Core Features\n\n### Data Features\n- **Real-world prompt dataset**: Contains prompts from various sources with toxicity annotations\n- **Naughty words list**: Curated list of potentially offensive terms (`data/list_of_naughty_and_bad_words.txt`)\n- **Webtext processing**: Tools to handle large-scale text data\n\n### Generation Capabilities\n- Multiple generation methods:\n  - Standard GPT-2 (`gpt2_generation.py`)\n  - PPLM (Plug and Play Language Models) variant (`pplm_generation.py`)\n- Control mechanisms for generation parameters\n\n### Toxicity Analysis\n- Integration with Perspective API for toxicity scoring\n- Notebooks for analyzing generation results (`notebooks/realtoxicityprompts_results.ipynb`)\n- Comparative analysis tools\n\n### Model Customization\n- Fine-tuning scripts for:\n  - Affect language models (`finetune_affect_lm.py`)\n  - Controlled GPT-2 generation (`finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py`)\n\n## Usage\nThe project is designed to be run in a Conda environment (`rtp`) with all dependencies specified in `environment.yml`. Main entry points are:\n- `scripts/run_prompts_experiment.py` for running generation experiments\n- Jupyter notebooks for analysis\n- Individual scripts for specific data processing tasks\n\n## Dependencies\nKey external dependencies include:\n- Perspective API (for toxicity scoring)\n- OpenAI API (for GPT-3 access)\n- PyTorch (for model implementations)\n- Transformers library (for language models)\n\nThe project provides a comprehensive framework for studying toxicity in language model generations, with particular attention to real-world scenarios and controlled experiments.\n\n3. 2025-09-18 23:33:21,374 - tools.graph_constructor - DEBUG - Technique: Vocabulary Shifting (AffectGPT2), A decoding-based detoxification technique that learns a 2-dimensional representation of toxicity and non-toxicity for every token in the model's vocabulary. This representation is used to boost the likelihood of non-toxic tokens during generation. The method involves training a projection layer on a balanced subsample of non-toxic and toxic corpora, then using this layer to adjust the model's vocabulary logits during generation by adding a term that encodes the desired toxicity level.

# Task
Your task is to transform a collection of disparate source code snippets, which are the official implementation of a technique component from a research paper RealToxicityPrompts: Evaluating Neural Toxic Degeneration in Language  Models, into a single, self-contained, and executable code block. The final code block must be clean, well-documented, and easy for others to understand and run.

# Input
1. Abstract of the paper RealToxicityPrompts: Evaluating Neural Toxic Degeneration in Language  Models:
Pretrained neural language models (LMs) are prone to generating racist,
sexist, or otherwise toxic language which hinders their safe deployment. We
investigate the extent to which pretrained LMs can be prompted to generate
toxic language, and the effectiveness of controllable text generation
algorithms at preventing such toxic degeneration. We create and release
RealToxicityPrompts, a dataset of 100K naturally occurring, sentence-level
prompts derived from a large corpus of English web text, paired with toxicity
scores from a widely-used toxicity classifier. Using RealToxicityPrompts, we
find that pretrained LMs can degenerate into toxic text even from seemingly
innocuous prompts. We empirically assess several controllable generation
methods, and find that while data- or compute-intensive methods (e.g., adaptive
pretraining on non-toxic data) are more effective at steering away from
toxicity than simpler solutions (e.g., banning "bad" words), no current method
is failsafe against neural toxic degeneration. To pinpoint the potential cause
of such persistent toxic degeneration, we analyze two web text corpora used to
pretrain several LMs (including GPT-2; Radford et. al, 2019), and find a
significant amount of offensive, factually unreliable, and otherwise toxic
content. Our work provides a test bed for evaluating toxic generations by LMs
and stresses the need for better data selection processes for pretraining.

2. Technical Concept Definition from the paper RealToxicityPrompts: Evaluating Neural Toxic Degeneration in Language  Models:
{
  "name": "Non-Toxic Corpus Selection",
  "description": "Selects the bottom 2 percentiles of documents by toxicity from the OpenWebText corpus to create a non-toxic training set. This subset is used for domain-adaptive pretraining to steer the model towards generating less toxic text."
}

3. Relevant Code Files:
[{"file_name": "scripts/data/create_prompts_dataset.py", "code_list": ["from pathlib import Path\nfrom typing import Union\n\nimport pandas as pd\nimport spacy\nfrom spacy.tokens.doc import Doc\nfrom sqlalchemy.sql.functions import random\n\nfrom utils.constants import TEXTS_DIR, OPENWEBTEXT_DB\nfrom utils.db import SpanScore, corpus_db_session\nimport click\n\n# Span constants\nMIN_SPAN_LEN = 64\nMAX_SPAN_LEN = 1024\nMAX_PROMPT_LEN = 128\n\n\ndef split_prompt(doc: Doc, n: int):\n    if isinstance(n, float):\n        # TODO: round n rather than flooring it\n        n = round(n * len(doc))\n\n    # Split text into prompt and continuation\n    prompt = str(doc[:n])\n    continuation = str(doc)[len(prompt):]  # Rather than taking remaining tokens, take the remainder of the string\n    if len(prompt) == 0 or len(continuation) == 0 or len(prompt) > MAX_PROMPT_LEN:\n        return None\n\n    return prompt, continuation\n\n\ndef load_span_example(row: pd.Series, n: Union[int, float], nlp):\n    # Load text from file\n    text_file = TEXTS_DIR / row.filename\n    try:\n        text = text_file.read_text(encoding='utf-8', errors='strict')\n    except UnicodeDecodeError:\n        return None\n\n    # Trim text\n    text = text[row.begin:row.end].strip()\n    if not (MIN_SPAN_LEN <= len(text) <= MAX_SPAN_LEN):\n        return None\n\n    # Tokenize text\n    doc = nlp(text)\n    return split_prompt(doc, n)\n\n\n@click.command()\n@click.option('--out_file', required=True, type=str)\n@click.option('--n', required=True, type=float)\ndef create_prompts_dataset(out_file: str, n: float):\n    out_file = Path(out_file)\n    if out_file.exists():\n        raise FileExistsError(\"Output file already exists.\")\n    if not OPENWEBTEXT_DB.exists():\n        raise FileNotFoundError(\"Perspective database was not found.\")\n\n    session = corpus_db_session()\n\n    # TODO: do this for all four ranges of toxicity\n    query = (\n        session.query(SpanScore)\n            .filter(SpanScore.toxicity < 0.25)\n            .filter(SpanScore.end - SpanScore.begin >= MIN_SPAN_LEN)\n            .filter(SpanScore.end - SpanScore.begin <= MAX_SPAN_LEN)\n            .order_by(random())\n            .limit(30_000)\n    )\n\n    # Load dataframe from query and select relevant columns\n    print(\"Reading from database...\")\n    df = pd.read_sql(query.statement, con=query.session.bind)\n    df = df[['filename', 'begin', 'end', 'toxicity']]\n    print(f\"Returned {len(df)} rows\")\n\n    # Get prompts and continuations\n    print(\"Loading text and tokenizing...\")\n    nlp = spacy.load('en_core_web_sm', disable=['parser', 'ner', 'tagger'])\n    examples = df.apply(lambda row: load_span_example(row, n, nlp), axis=1)\n\n    # Add prompts and continuations to dataframe\n    df = df[examples.notna()]\n    df['prompt'], df['continuation'] = zip(*examples.dropna())\n    print(f'Limited to {len(df)} rows after preprocessing')\n\n    df = df.head(25_000)\n\n    df.to_pickle(out_file)\n    return df\n\n\nif __name__ == '__main__':\n    create_prompts_dataset()\n"]}]

2025-09-18 23:33:38,982 - llm.llm_def - DEBUG - OpenAI query completed in 17.61s Response: ```python
import pandas as pd
from typing import Optional

def select_non_toxic_corpus(df: pd.DataFrame, toxicity_column: str = 'toxicity', quantile: float = 0.02) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """
    Selects non-toxic documents from the provided DataFrame by filtering documents whose toxicity 
    scores lie within the bottom specified quantile. This function calculates the toxicity threshold 
    corresponding to the desired quantile (e.g., 0.02 for the bottom 2 percentiles) and returns a subset 
    of the DataFrame with documents having toxicity scores less than or equal to that threshold.
    
    Parameters:
        df (pd.DataFrame): A DataFrame containing the documents with associated toxicity scores.
        toxicity_column (str): The name of the column in the DataFrame that holds the toxicity scores.
        quantile (float): The quantile (between 0 and 1) used to filter out the bottom documents 
                          based on toxicity. The default value is 0.02 for the bottom 2 percentiles.
    
    Returns:
        pd.DataFrame: A new DataFrame containing only the documents with toxicity scores within the 
                      specified non-toxic range.
                      
    Raises:
        ValueError: If the specified toxicity column is not found in the provided DataFrame.
    """
    if toxicity_column not in df.columns:
        raise ValueError(f"Column '{toxicity_column}' not found in the DataFrame.")
        
    threshold = df[toxicity_column].quantile(quantile)
    non_toxic_df = df[df[toxicity_column] <= threshold].copy()
    return non_toxic_df

# TEST BLOCK
if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Sample dataset simulating documents with assigned toxicity scores.
    data = {
        'text': [
            "This is a harmless sentence.",
            "This sentence is a bit controversial.",
            "An extremely non-toxic document.",
            "Mildly toxic content might appear here.",
            "Another very safe and non-toxic document."
        ],
        'toxicity': [0.1, 0.15, 0.02, 0.2, 0.03]
    }
    df = pd.DataFrame(data)
    
    # Note: With a small dataset, using a quantile of 0.4 (i.e., bottom 40%) for demonstration purposes.
    # The original technique selects the bottom 2 percentiles, but a higher quantile is used here to ensure example viability.
    selected_non_toxic = select_non_toxic_corpus(df, quantile=0.4)
    
    print("Selected Non-Toxic Documents:")
    print(selected_non_toxic)
```

```
This module implements a non-toxic corpus selection technique that is designed to extract documents with low toxicity scores from a dataset. The core function, select_non_toxic_corpus, computes the toxicity threshold corresponding to a specified quantile (by default, the bottom 2 percentiles) and filters the dataset accordingly. The function accepts a Pandas DataFrame with a column for toxicity scores and verifies that the column exists. It then returns a refined DataFrame containing only those documents below the calculated toxicity threshold. The provided test block demonstrates the usage of this function with a sample dataset and a modified quantile for clarity. This approach is useful for preparing a non-toxic training set for domain-adaptive pretraining, as outlined in the related research paper....

# Task
Your task is to transform a collection of disparate source code snippets, which are the official implementation of a technique component from a research paper RealToxicityPrompts: Evaluating Neural Toxic Degeneration in Language  Models, into a single, self-contained, and executable code block. The final code block must be clean, well-documented, and easy for others to understand and run.

# Input
1. Abstract of the paper RealToxicityPrompts: Evaluating Neural Toxic Degeneration in Language  Models:
Pretrained neural language models (LMs) are prone to generating racist,
sexist, or otherwise toxic language which hinders their safe deployment. We
investigate the extent to which pretrained LMs can be prompted to generate
toxic language, and the effectiveness of controllable text generation
algorithms at preventing such toxic degeneration. We create and release
RealToxicityPrompts, a dataset of 100K naturally occurring, sentence-level
prompts derived from a large corpus of English web text, paired with toxicity
scores from a widely-used toxicity classifier. Using RealToxicityPrompts, we
find that pretrained LMs can degenerate into toxic text even from seemingly
innocuous prompts. We empirically assess several controllable generation
methods, and find that while data- or compute-intensive methods (e.g., adaptive
pretraining on non-toxic data) are more effective at steering away from
toxicity than simpler solutions (e.g., banning "bad" words), no current method
is failsafe against neural toxic degeneration. To pinpoint the potential cause
of such persistent toxic degeneration, we analyze two web text corpora used to
pretrain several LMs (including GPT-2; Radford et. al, 2019), and find a
significant amount of offensive, factually unreliable, and otherwise toxic
content. Our work provides a test bed for evaluating toxic generations by LMs
and stresses the need for better data selection processes for pretraining.

2. Technical Concept Definition from the paper RealToxicityPrompts: Evaluating Neural Toxic Degeneration in Language  Models:
{
  "name": "Fine-Tuning on Non-Toxic Data",
  "description": "The pretrained language model is fine-tuned on the selected non-toxic corpus to adapt its behavior. This involves training the model with standard language modeling objectives on the non-toxic data to reduce its propensity for toxic generations."
}

3. Relevant Code Files:
[{"file_name": "scripts/finetuning/finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py", "code_list": ["# coding=utf-8\n# Copyright 2018 The Google AI Language Team Authors and The HuggingFace Inc. team.\n# Copyright (c) 2018, NVIDIA CORPORATION.  All rights reserved.\n#\n# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the \"License\");\n# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.\n# You may obtain a copy of the License at\n#\n#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0\n#\n# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software\n# distributed under the License is distributed on an \"AS IS\" BASIS,\n# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.\n# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and\n# limitations under the License.\n\"\"\"\nFine-tuning the library models for language modeling on a text file (GPT, GPT-2, BERT, RoBERTa).\nGPT and GPT-2 are fine-tuned using a causal language modeling (CLM) loss while BERT and RoBERTa are fine-tuned\nusing a masked language modeling (MLM) loss.\n\"\"\"\n\nimport logging\nimport math\nimport os\nimport pickle\nimport time\nfrom dataclasses import dataclass, field\nfrom typing import Optional, List\n\nimport torch\nfrom torch.utils.data import Dataset, ConcatDataset\nfrom tqdm import trange\nfrom transformers import (\n    CONFIG_MAPPING,\n    MODEL_WITH_LM_HEAD_MAPPING,\n    AutoConfig,\n    AutoModelWithLMHead,\n    AutoTokenizer,\n    DataCollatorForLanguageModeling,\n    HfArgumentParser,\n    PreTrainedTokenizer,\n    Trainer,\n    TrainingArguments,\n    set_seed,\n    torch_distributed_zero_first,\n)\n\nlogger = logging.getLogger(__name__)\n\nMODEL_CONFIG_CLASSES = list(MODEL_WITH_LM_HEAD_MAPPING.keys())\nMODEL_TYPES = tuple(conf.model_type for conf in MODEL_CONFIG_CLASSES)\n\n\nclass CTRLTextDataset(Dataset):\n    def __init__(\n            self, tokenizer: PreTrainedTokenizer, file_path: str, block_size: int, ctrl_code_tok: int,\n            overwrite_cache=False, local_rank=-1,\n    ):\n        assert os.path.isfile(file_path)\n\n        # Subtract 1 so we can prepend the CTRL code\n        block_size = block_size - tokenizer.num_special_tokens_to_add(pair=False) - 1\n\n        directory, filename = os.path.split(file_path)\n        cached_features_file = os.path.join(\n            directory, \"cached_lm_{}_{}_{}\".format(tokenizer.__class__.__name__, str(block_size), filename, ),\n        )\n\n        with torch_distributed_zero_first(local_rank):\n            # Make sure only the first process in distributed training processes the dataset,\n            # and the others will use the cache.\n\n            if os.path.exists(cached_features_file) and not overwrite_cache:\n                start = time.time()\n                with open(cached_features_file, \"rb\") as handle:\n                    self.examples = pickle.load(handle)\n                logger.info(\n                    f\"Loading features from cached file {cached_features_file} [took %.3f s]\", time.time() - start\n                )\n\n            else:\n                logger.info(f\"Creating features from dataset file at {directory}\")\n\n                self.examples = []\n                with open(file_path, encoding=\"utf-8\") as f:\n                    text = f.read()\n\n                tokenized_text = tokenizer.convert_tokens_to_ids(tokenizer.tokenize(text))\n\n                for i in trange(0, len(tokenized_text) - block_size + 1, block_size):  # Truncate in block of block_size\n                    self.examples.append(\n                        tokenizer.build_inputs_with_special_tokens([ctrl_code_tok] + tokenized_text[i: i + block_size])\n                    )\n                # Note that we are losing the last truncated example here for the sake of simplicity (no padding)\n                # If your dataset is small, first you should loook for a bigger one :-) and second you\n                # can change this behavior by adding (model specific) padding.\n\n                start = time.time()\n                with open(cached_features_file, \"wb\") as handle:\n                    pickle.dump(self.examples, handle, protocol=pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL)\n                logger.info(\n                    f\"Saving features into cached file %s [took %.3f s]\", cached_features_file, time.time() - start\n                )\n\n    def __len__(self):\n        return len(self.examples)\n\n    def __getitem__(self, i) -> torch.Tensor:\n        return torch.tensor(self.examples[i], dtype=torch.long)\n\n\n@dataclass\nclass ModelArguments:\n    \"\"\"\n    Arguments pertaining to which model/config/tokenizer we are going to fine-tune, or train from scratch.\n    \"\"\"\n\n    model_name_or_path: Optional[str] = field(\n        default=None,\n        metadata={\n            \"help\": \"The model checkpoint for weights initialization. DataTrainingArguments:\n    \"\"\"\n    Arguments pertaining to what data we are going to input our model for training and eval.\n    \"\"\"\n\n    train_data_files: Optional[str] = field(\n after tokenization.\"\n                    \"The training dataset will be truncated in block of this size for training.\"\n                    \"Default to the model max input length for single sentence inputs (take into account special tokens).\"\n        },\n    )\n    overwrite_cache: bool = field(\n        default=False, metadata={\"help\": \"Overwrite the cached training and evaluation sets\"}\n    )\n\n\ndef get_dataset(args: DataTrainingArguments, tokenizer: PreTrainedTokenizer, ctrl_codes: List[int], evaluate=False,\n                local_rank=-1):\n    file_paths = args.eval_data_files if evaluate else args.train_data_files\n    datasets = []\n    for file_path, ctrl_code in zip(file_paths, ctrl_codes):\n        d = CTRLTextDataset(tokenizer=tokenizer, We\ninvestigate the extent to which pretrained LMs can be prompted to generate\ntoxic language, and the effectiveness of controllable text generation\nalgorithms at preventing such toxic degeneration. We create and release\nRealToxicityPrompts, a dataset of 100K naturally occurring, sentence-level\nprompts derived from a large corpus of English web text, paired with toxicity\nscores from a widely-used toxicity classifier. Using RealToxicityPrompts, we\nfind that pretrained LMs can degenerate into toxic text even from seemingly\ninnocuous prompts. We empirically assess several controllable generation\nmethods, and find that while data- or compute-intensive methods (e.g., adaptive\npretraining on non-toxic data) are more effective at steering away from\ntoxicity than simpler solutions (e.g., banning "bad" words), no current method\nis failsafe against neural toxic degeneration. All rights reserved.\\n#\\n# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the \\"License\\");\\n# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.\\n# You may obtain a copy of the License at\\n#\\n#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0\\n#\\n# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software\\n# distributed under the License is distributed on an \\"AS IS\\" BASIS,\\n# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.\\n# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and\\n# limitations under the License.\\n\\"\\"\\"\\nFine-tuning the library models for language modeling on a text file (GPT, GPT-2, BERT, RoBERTa).\\nGPT and GPT-2 are fine-tuned using a causal language modeling (CLM) loss while BERT and RoBERTa are fine-tuned\\nusing a masked language modeling (MLM) loss.\\n\\"\\"\\"\\n\\nimport logging\\nimport math\\nimport os\\nimport pickle\\nimport time\\nfrom dataclasses import dataclass, field\\nfrom typing import Optional, List\\n\\nimport torch\\nfrom torch.utils.data import Dataset, ConcatDataset\\nfrom tqdm import trange\\nfrom transformers import (\\n    CONFIG_MAPPING,\\n    MODEL_WITH_LM_HEAD_MAPPING,\\n    AutoConfig,\\n    AutoModelWithLMHead,\\n    AutoTokenizer,\\n    DataCollatorForLanguageModeling,\\n    HfArgumentParser,\\n    PreTrainedTokenizer,\\n    Trainer,\\n    TrainingArguments,\\n    set_seed,\\n    torch_distributed_zero_first,\\n)\\n\\nlogger = logging.getLogger(__name__)\\n\\nMODEL_CONFIG_CLASSES = list(MODEL_WITH_LM_HEAD_MAPPING.keys())\\nMODEL_TYPES = tuple(conf.model_type for conf in MODEL_CONFIG_CLASSES)\\n\\n\\nclass CTRLTextDataset(Dataset):\\n    def __init__(\\n            self, tokenizer: PreTrainedTokenizer, file_path: str, block_size: int, ctrl_code_tok: int,\\n            overwrite_cache=False, local_rank=-1,\\n    ):\\n        assert os.path.isfile(file_path)\\n\\n        # Subtract 1 so we can prepend the CTRL code\\n        block_size = block_size - tokenizer.num_special_tokens_to_add(pair=False) - 1\\n\\n        directory, filename = os.path.split(file_path)\\n        cached_features_file = os.path.join(\\n            directory, \\"cached_lm_{}_{}_{}\\".format(tokenizer.__class__.__name__, str(block_size), filename, ),\\n        )\\n\\n        with torch_distributed_zero_first(local_rank):\\n            # Make sure only the first process in distributed training processes the dataset,\\n            # and the others will use the cache.\\n\\n            if os.path.exists(cached_features_file) and not overwrite_cache:\\n                start = time.time()\\n                with open(cached_features_file, \\"rb\\") as handle:\\n                    self.examples = pickle.load(handle)\\n                logger.info(\\n                    f\\"Loading features from cached file {cached_features_file} [took %.3f s]\\", time.time() - start\\n                )\\n\\n            else:\\n                logger.info(f\\"Creating features from dataset file at {directory}\\")\\n\\n                self.examples = []\\n                with open(file_path, encoding=\\"utf-8\\") as f:\\n                    text = f.read()\\n\\n                tokenized_text = tokenizer.convert_tokens_to_ids(tokenizer.tokenize(text))\\n\\n                for i in trange(0, len(tokenized_text) - block_size + 1, block_size):  # Truncate in block of block_size\\n                    self.examples.append(\\n                        tokenizer.build_inputs_with_special_tokens([ctrl_code_tok] + tokenized_text[i: i + block_size])\\n                    )\\n                # Note that we are losing the last truncated example here for the sake of simplicity (no padding)\\n                # If your dataset is small, first you should loook for a bigger one :-) and second you\\n                # can change this behavior by adding (model specific) padding.\\n\\n                start = time.time()\\n                with open(cached_features_file, \\"wb\\") as handle:\\n                    pickle.dump(self.examples, handle, protocol=pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL)\\n                logger.info(\\n                    f\\"Saving features into cached file %s [took %.3f s]\\", cached_features_file, time.time() - start\\n                )\\n\\n    def __len__(self):\\n        return len(self.examples)\\n\\n    def __getitem__(self, i) -> torch.Tensor:\\n        return torch.tensor(self.examples[i], dtype=torch.long)\\n\\n\\n@dataclass\\nclass ModelArguments:\\n    \\"\\"\\"\\n    Arguments pertaining to which model/config/tokenizer we are going to fine-tune, or train from scratch.\\n    \\"\\"\\"\\n\\n    model_name_or_path: Optional[str] = field(\\n        default=None,\\n        metadata={\\n            \\"help\\": \\"The model checkpoint for weights initialization. Leave None if you want to train a model from scratch.\\"\\n        },\\n    )\\n    model_type: Optional[str] = field(\\n        default=None,\\n        metadata={\\"help\\": \\"If training from scratch, pass a model type from the list: \\" + \\", \\".join(MODEL_TYPES)},\\n    )\\n    config_name: Optional[str] = field(\\n        default=None, metadata={\\"help\\": \\"Pretrained config name or path if not the same as model_name\\"}\\n    )\\n    tokenizer_name: Optional[str] = field(\\n        default=None, metadata={\\"help\\": \\"Pretrained tokenizer name or path if not the same as model_name\\"}\\n    )\\n    cache_dir: Optional[str] = field(\\n        default=None, metadata={\\"help\\": \\"Where do you want to store the pretrained models downloaded from s3\\"}\\n    )\\n\\n\\n@dataclass\\nclass DataTrainingArguments:\\n    \\"\\"\\"\\n    Arguments pertaining to what data we are going to input our model for training and eval.\\n    \\"\\"\\"\\n\\n    train_data_files: Optional[str] = field(\\n        default=None,\\n        metadata={\\n            \\"nargs\\": \\"+\\",\\n        }\\n    )\\n    eval_data_files: Optional[str] = field(\\n        default=None,\\n        metadata={\\n            \\"nargs\\": \\"+\\",\\n        },\\n    )\\n    ctrl_codes: Optional[str] = field(\\n        default=None,\\n        metadata={\\n            \\"nargs\\": \\"+\\"\\n        }\\n    )\\n\\n    mlm: bool = field(\\n        default=False, metadata={\\"help\\": \\"Train with masked-language modeling loss instead of language modeling.\\"}\\n    )\\n    mlm_probability: float = field(\\n        default=0.15, metadata={\\"help\\": \\"Ratio of tokens to mask for masked language modeling loss\\"}\\n    )\\n\\n    block_size: int = field(\\n        default=-1,\\n        metadata={\\n            \\"help\\": \\"Optional input sequence length after tokenization.\\"\\n                    \\"The training dataset will be truncated in block of this size for training.\\"\\n                    \\"Default to the model max input length for single sentence inputs (take into account special tokens).\\"\\n        },\\n    )\\n    overwrite_cache: bool = field(\\n        default=False, metadata={\\"help\\": \\"Overwrite the cached training and evaluation sets\\"}\\n    )\\n\\n\\ndef get_dataset(args: DataTrainingArguments, tokenizer: PreTrainedTokenizer, ctrl_codes: List[int], evaluate=False,\\n                local_rank=-1):\\n    file_paths = args.eval_data_files if evaluate else args.train_data_files\\n    datasets = []\\n    for file_path, ctrl_code in zip(file_paths, ctrl_codes):\\n        d = CTRLTextDataset(tokenizer=tokenizer, file_path=file_path, block_size=args.block_size,\\n                            ctrl_code_tok=ctrl_code, local_rank=local_rank)\\n        datasets.append(d)\\n    return ConcatDataset(datasets)\\n\\n\\ndef main():\\n    # See all possible arguments in None and training_args.do_eval:\\n        raise ValueError(\\n            \\"Cannot do evaluation without an evaluation data file. Either supply a file to --eval_data_file \\"\\n            \\"or remove the --do_eval argument.\\"\\n        )\\n\\n    if (\\n            os.path.exists(training_args.output_dir)\\n            and os.listdir(training_args.output_dir)\\n            and training_args.do_train\\n            and not training_args.overwrite_output_dir\\n    ):\\n        raise ValueError(\\n            f\\"Output directory ({training_args.output_dir}) already exists and is not empty. Use --overwrite_output_dir to overcome.\\"\\n        )\\n\\n    # Setup logging\\n    logging.basicConfig(\\n        format=\\"%(asctime)s - %(levelname)s - %(name)s -   %(message)s\\",\\n        datefmt=\\"%m/%d/%Y %H:%M:%S\\",\\n        level=logging.INFO if training_args.local_rank in [-1, 0] else logging.WARN,\\n    )\\n    logger.warning(\\n        \\"Process rank: %s, device: %s, n_gpu: %s, distributed training: %s, 16-bits training: %s\\",\\n        training_args.local_rank,\\n        training_args.device,\\n        training_args.n_gpu,\\n        bool(training_args.local_rank != -1),\\n        training_args.fp16,\\n    )\\n    logger.info(\\"Training/evaluation parameters %s\\", training_args)\\n\\n    # Set seed\\n    set_seed(training_args.seed)\\n\\n    # Load pretrained model and tokenizer\\n    #\\n    # Distributed training:\\n    # The .from_pretrained methods guarantee that only one local process can concurrently\\n    # download model & vocab.\\n\\n    if model_args.config_name:\\n        config = AutoConfig.from_pretrained(model_args.config_name, cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir)\\n    elif model_args.model_name_or_path:\\n        config = AutoConfig.from_pretrained(model_args.model_name_or_path, cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir)\\n    else:\\n        config = CONFIG_MAPPING[model_args.model_type]()\\n        logger.warning(\\"You are instantiating a new config instance from scratch.\\")\\n\\n    if model_args.tokenizer_name:\\n        tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_args.tokenizer_name, cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir)\\n    elif model_args.model_name_or_path:\\n        tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_args.model_name_or_path, cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir)\\n    else:\\n        raise ValueError(\\n            \\"You are instantiating a new tokenizer from scratch. This is not supported, but you can do it from another script, save it,\\"\\n            \\"and load it from here, using --tokenizer_name\\"\\n        )\\n\\n    # Add CTRL codes to vocab\\n    assert len(data_args.ctrl_codes) == len(data_args.eval_data_files) == len(data_args.ctrl_codes)\\n    print(\\"CTRL codes:\\", *data_args.ctrl_codes)\\n    num_added_toks = tokenizer.add_tokens(data_args.ctrl_codes)\\n    assert num_added_toks == len(data_args.ctrl_codes)\\n    ctrl_code_toks = tokenizer.encode(data_args.ctrl_codes)\\n\\n    if model_args.model_name_or_path:\\n        model = AutoModelWithLMHead.from_pretrained(\\n            model_args.model_name_or_path,\\n            from_tf=bool(\\".ckpt\\" in model_args.model_name_or_path),\\n            config=config,\\n            cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir,\\n        )\\n    else:\\n        logger.info(\\"Training new model from scratch\\")\\n        model = AutoModelWithLMHead.from_config(config)\\n\\n    model.resize_token_embeddings(len(tokenizer))\\n\\n    if config.model_type in [\\"bert\\", \\"roberta\\", \\"distilbert\\", \\"camembert\\"] and not data_args.mlm:\\n        raise ValueError(\\n            \\"BERT and RoBERTa-like models do not have LM heads but masked LM heads. They must be run using the --mlm \\"\\n            \\"flag (masked language modeling).\\"\\n        )\\n\\n    if data_args.block_size <= 0:\\n        data_args.block_size = tokenizer.max_len\\n        # Our input block size will be the max possible for the model\\n    else:\\n        data_args.block_size = min(data_args.block_size, tokenizer.max_len)\\n\\n    # Get datasets\\n    train_dataset = (\\n        get_dataset(data_args, tokenizer=tokenizer, local_rank=training_args.local_rank, ctrl_codes=ctrl_code_toks)\\n        if training_args.do_train\\n        else None\\n    )\\n    eval_dataset = (\\n        get_dataset(data_args, tokenizer=tokenizer, local_rank=training_args.local_rank, evaluate=True, ctrl_codes=ctrl_code_toks)\\n        if training_args.do_eval\\n        else None\\n    )\\n    data_collator = DataCollatorForLanguageModeling(\\n        tokenizer=tokenizer, mlm=data_args.mlm, mlm_probability=data_args.mlm_probability\\n    )\\n\\n    # Initialize our Trainer\\n    trainer = Trainer(\\n        model=model,\\n        args=training_args,\\n        data_collator=data_collator,\\n        train_dataset=train_dataset,\\n        eval_dataset=eval_dataset,\\n        prediction_loss_only=True,\\n    )\\n\\n    # For convenience, we also re-save the tokenizer to the same directory,\\n        # so that you can share your model easily on huggingface.co/models =)\\n        if trainer.is_world_master():\\n            tokenizer.save_pretrained(training_args.output_dir)\\n\\n    # Evaluation\\n    results = {}\\n    if training_args.do_eval and training_args.local_rank in [-1, 0]:\\n        logger.info(\\"*** Evaluate ***\\")\\n\\n        eval_output = trainer.evaluate()\\n\\n        perplexity = math.exp(eval_output[\\"loss\\"])\\n        result = {\\"perplexity\\": perplexity}\\n\\n        output_eval_file = os.path.join(training_args.output_dir, \\"eval_results_lm.txt\\")\\n        with open(output_eval_file, \\"w\\") as writer:\\n            logger.info(\\"***** Eval results *****\\")\\n            for key in sorted(result.keys()):\\n                logger.info(\\"  %s = %s\\", key, str(result[key]))\\n                writer.write(\\"%s = %s\\\\n\\" % (key, str(result[key])))\\n\\n        results.update(result)\\n\\n    return results\\n\\n\\nif __name__ == \\"__main__\\":\\n    main()\\n"]}, {"file_name": "scripts/finetuning/finetune_affect_lm.py", "code_list": ["# coding=utf-8\\n# Copyright 2018 The Google AI Language Team Authors and The HuggingFace Inc. team.\\n# Copyright (c) 2018, NVIDIA CORPORATION. #!/usr/bin/env python
"""
This module implements fine-tuning a pretrained language model on a non‐toxic corpus.
The core technique follows the "Fine-Tuning on Non-Toxic Data" approach from
RealToxicityPrompts, where a pretrained language model is adapted using standard
language modeling objectives on a selected non-toxic text file. The dataset class
reads a text file, tokenizes the content, and splits it into fixed-length blocks,
optionally caching the processed data for faster subsequent runs. The Trainer
from Hugging Face’s transformers library is used to handle the training loop,
including loss calculation and model updates. The model, tokenizer, and training
parameters are configurable via function arguments or command-line inputs.
This self-contained script encapsulates the fine-tuning process and includes a test
block demonstrating how to run training on a default non-toxic text file.
"""

import os
import pickle
import time
import logging
import math
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Optional, Any

import torch
from torch.utils.data import Dataset

from transformers import (
    AutoConfig,
    AutoModelForCausalLM,
    AutoTokenizer,
    DataCollatorForLanguageModeling,
    Trainer,
    TrainingArguments,
    HfArgumentParser,
    set_seed,
)

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
logging.basicConfig(format="%(asctime)s - %(levelname)s - %(name)s - %(message)s", level=logging.INFO)


class NonToxicDataset(Dataset):
    """
    A dataset for fine-tuning a language model on non-toxic data.
    It reads a text file, tokenizes the content, and splits it into blocks.
    Optionally, it caches the tokenized examples to avoid reprocessing.
    """

    def __init__(self, tokenizer: Any, file_path: str, block_size: int, overwrite_cache: bool = False) -> None:
        """
        Initializes the dataset.

        Args:
            tokenizer (PreTrainedTokenizer): The tokenizer used for encoding the text.
            file_path (str): Path to the text file containing non-toxic data.
            block_size (int): Maximum number of tokens per example.
            overwrite_cache (bool): Whether to overwrite the cached dataset.
        """
        if not os.path.isfile(file_path):
            raise ValueError(f"Input file path {file_path} not found")

        # Adjust block size to account for special tokens
        block_size = block_size - tokenizer.num_special_tokens_to_add(pair=False)

        cache_file = file_path + f".cached.block_size_{block_size}"
        if os.path.exists(cache_file) and not overwrite_cache:
            start = time.time()
            with open(cache_file, "rb") as handle:
                self.examples = pickle.load(handle)
            logger.info(f"Loaded features from cached file {cache_file} (took {time.time() - start:.3f} s)")
        else:
            logger.info(f"Creating features from dataset file at {file_path}")
            self.examples = []
            with open(file_path, encoding="utf-8") as f:
                text = f.read()

            tokenized_text = tokenizer.convert_tokens_to_ids(tokenizer.tokenize(text))
            # Split into blocks of block_size tokens
            for i in range(0, len(tokenized_text) - block_size + 1, block_size):
                block = tokenized_text[i: i + block_size]
                # Build model input using special tokens (e.g., adding BOS/EOS if necessary)
                self.examples.append(tokenizer.build_inputs_with_special_tokens(block))
            # Cache the processed dataset
            start = time.time()
            with open(cache_file, "wb") as handle:
                pickle.dump(self.examples, handle, protocol=pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL)
            logger.info(f"Saved features into cached file {cache_file} (took {time.time() - start:.3f} s)")

    def __len__(self) -> int:
        """Returns the number of examples in the dataset."""
        return len(self.examples)

    def __getitem__(self, i: int) -> torch.Tensor:
        """
        Returns the i-th example as a torch.Tensor.

        Args:
            i (int): Index of the example.
        Returns:
            torch.Tensor: Tensor of token ids.
        """
        return torch.tensor(self.examples[i], dtype=torch.long)


@dataclass
class ModelArguments:
    """
    Arguments pertaining to which model/config/tokenizer we are going to use.
    """
    model_name_or_path: Optional[str] = field(
        default="gpt2",
        metadata={"help": "Path to pre-trained model or shortcut name"}
    )
    config_name: Optional[str] = field(
        default=None,
        metadata={"help": "Pretrained config name or path if not the same as model_name"}
    )
    tokenizer_name: Optional[str] = field(
        default=None,
        metadata={"help": "Pretrained tokenizer name or path if not the same as model_name"}
    )
    cache_dir: Optional[str] = field(
        default=None,
        metadata={"help": "Where to store downloaded pretrained models"}
    )


@dataclass
class DataTrainingArguments:
    """
    Arguments pertaining to data inputs for training.
    """
    train_data_file: Optional[str] = field(
        default="non_toxic_data.txt",
        metadata={"help": "The input training data file (a text file)."}
    )
    block_size: int = field(
        default=128,
        metadata={"help": "Optional input sequence length after tokenization."}
    )
    overwrite_cache: bool = field(
        default=False,
        metadata={"help": "Overwrite the cached training set"}
    )


def get_non_toxic_dataset(data_args: DataTrainingArguments, tokenizer: Any) -> NonToxicDataset:
    """
    Creates a dataset for non-toxic data fine-tuning.

    Args:
        data_args (DataTrainingArguments): Data related arguments.
        tokenizer (PreTrainedTokenizer): The tokenizer for encoding the data.

    Returns:
        NonToxicDataset: The processed dataset.
    """
    return NonToxicDataset(
        tokenizer=tokenizer,
        file_path=data_args.train_data_file,
        block_size=data_args.block_size,
        overwrite_cache=data_args.overwrite_cache,
    )


def fine_tune_non_toxic_model(model_args: ModelArguments, data_args: DataTrainingArguments, training_args: TrainingArguments) -> None:
    """
    Fine-tunes a pretrained language model on a non-toxic corpus.

    Args:
        model_args (ModelArguments): Model-related arguments.
        data_args (DataTrainingArguments): Dataset related arguments.
        training_args (TrainingArguments): Training related arguments.
    """
    # Set seed for reproducibility
    set_seed(training_args.seed)

    # Load configuration, tokenizer, and model
    if model_args.config_name:
        config = AutoConfig.from_pretrained(model_args.config_name, cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir)
    else:
        config = AutoConfig.from_pretrained(model_args.model_name_or_path, cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir)

    if model_args.tokenizer_name:
        tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_args.tokenizer_name, cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir)
    else:
        tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_args.model_name_or_path, cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir)

    # Make sure block_size does not exceed model max length
    if data_args.block_size <= 0:
        data_args.block_size = tokenizer.model_max_length
    else:
        data_args.block_size = min(data_args.block_size, tokenizer.model_max_length)

    # Load the pretrained model
    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
        model_args.model_name_or_path,
        config=config,
        cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir,
    )
    model.resize_token_embeddings(len(tokenizer))

    # Create the dataset and data collator
    train_dataset = get_non_toxic_dataset(data_args, tokenizer)
    data_collator = DataCollatorForLanguageModeling(tokenizer=tokenizer, mlm=False)

    # Initialize Trainer
    trainer = Trainer(
        model=model,
        args=training_args,
        data_collator=data_collator,
        train_dataset=train_dataset,
        prediction_loss_only=True,
    )

    # Start training
    trainer.train()
    trainer.save_model()
    tokenizer.save_pretrained(training_args.output_dir)

    # Optionally evaluate: here we compute perplexity on training data as a simple check.
    eval_loss = trainer.evaluate()["loss"]
    perplexity = math.exp(eval_loss)
    logger.info(f"Evaluation perplexity: {perplexity:.2f}")


# TEST BLOCK
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Example default arguments for fine-tuning on non-toxic data.
    # Create a sample non-toxic data file if it does not exist.
    sample_data_file = "non_toxic_data.txt"
    if not os.path.isfile(sample_data_file):
        with open(sample_data_file, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
            f.write("This is a sample non-toxic sentence. " * 100)

    # Parse arguments using HfArgumentParser for demonstration.
    parser = HfArgumentParser((ModelArguments, DataTrainingArguments, TrainingArguments))
    model_args, data_args, training_args = parser.parse_args_into_dataclasses(args=[
        "--model_name_or_path", "gpt2",
        "--train_data_file", sample_data_file,
        "--output_dir", "./non_toxic_model_output",
        "--num_train_epochs", "1",
        "--per_device_train_batch_size", "2",
        "--logging_steps", "10",
        "--save_steps", "50",
        "--seed", "42",
    ])

    fine_tune_non_toxic_model(model_args, data_args, training_args)
```

```
This module implements the fine-tuning of a pretrained language model on a non-toxic corpus, following the approach described in the RealToxicityPrompts paper. The NonToxicDataset class reads a text file, tokenizes it using a specified tokenizer, and splits the tokenized text into blocks of a configurable size with optional caching. The fine_tune_non_toxic_model function loads the model and tokenizer, prepares the dataset and collator, and uses Hugging Face’s Trainer to perform the training while ensuring reproducibility through seeding. Configurable options include the model path, training data file, block size, training hyperparameters (e.g., epochs, batch size), and output directory. A test block demonstrates usage by creating a sample data file if needed and parsing command-line arguments with default values to run a brief training session. The design encapsulates core logic in functions and data classes, making it easy to reuse and extend the fine-tuning procedure.
```...

# Task
Your task is to transform a collection of disparate source code snippets, which are the official implementation of a technique component from a research paper RealToxicityPrompts: Evaluating Neural Toxic Degeneration in Language  Models, into a single, self-contained, and executable code block. # Input
1. Abstract of the paper RealToxicityPrompts: Evaluating Neural Toxic Degeneration in Language  Models:
Pretrained neural language models (LMs) are prone to generating racist,
sexist, or otherwise toxic language which hinders their safe deployment. We
investigate the extent to which pretrained LMs can be prompted to generate
toxic language, and the effectiveness of controllable text generation
algorithms at preventing such toxic degeneration. We create and release
RealToxicityPrompts, a dataset of 100K naturally occurring, sentence-level
prompts derived from a large corpus of English web text, paired with toxicity
scores from a widely-used toxicity classifier. Using RealToxicityPrompts, we
find that pretrained LMs can degenerate into toxic text even from seemingly
innocuous prompts. We empirically assess several controllable generation
methods, and find that while data- or compute-intensive methods (e.g., adaptive
pretraining on non-toxic data) are more effective at steering away from
toxicity than simpler solutions (e.g., banning "bad" words), no current method
is failsafe against neural toxic degeneration. To pinpoint the potential cause
of such persistent toxic degeneration, we analyze two web text corpora used to
pretrain several LMs (including GPT-2; Radford et. al, 2019), and find a
significant amount of offensive, factually unreliable, and otherwise toxic
content. Our work provides a test bed for evaluating toxic generations by LMs
and stresses the need for better data selection processes for pretraining.

2. Technical Concept Definition from the paper RealToxicityPrompts: Evaluating Neural Toxic Degeneration in Language  Models:
{
  "name": "Attribute Token Addition",
  "description": "Adds toxicity attribute tokens to the model's vocabulary to enable conditioning on toxicity levels during generation."
}

# Requirements
1. Dependency Management: Ensure all necessary imports and dependencies are included at the beginning of the code block.
2. Fidelity to the Original Technique: Strictly follow the description of the given technique to organize the code. ONLY focus on the implementation that DIRECTLY corresponds to THIS technique!!! (e.g., if the technique is a loss function definition, implement only the code for its calculation. Ignore all other parts of the algorithm's implementation, even if provided in the code snippets.)
3. Code Encapsulation and Documentation:
- Encapsulate the core logic of the technique into one or more functions/classes.
- Every function and class method must include a comprehensive docstring explaining its purpose, parameters, and return values.
- All function arguments and return values must have clear type hints.
- Preserve original parameters and comments from the source code.
4. Reproducibility and Testing:
- A main execution block, start with the comment `# TEST BLOCK`, is required at the end of the file, which serves as a practical usage example and a test case.
- The test case should use parameters from the code repository or paper. If missing, create and state your own defaults.
5. Fidelity to the Original Logic: 
- You must strictly adhere to the algorithmic logic present in the provided code snippets. Your role is to refactor and structure, not to re-implement or invent new logic.
- Minimal, necessary modifications are permitted (e.g., renaming variables for clarity, adapting function signatures for dependency injection), but the core computational steps must remain identical to the original author's implementation.
6. Documentation of Usage Scenarios: Provide a concise and fluent document of the code module’s core logic, configurable options, and usage. Limit the description to 5-10 clear and coherent sentences.
