Abstract of the paper RealToxicityPrompts: Evaluating Neural Toxic Degeneration in Language  Models:\nPretrained neural language models (LMs) are prone to generating racist,\nsexist, or otherwise toxic language which hinders their safe deployment. We\ninvestigate the extent to which pretrained LMs can be prompted to generate\ntoxic language, and the effectiveness of controllable text generation\nalgorithms at preventing such toxic degeneration. We create and release\nRealToxicityPrompts, a dataset of 100K naturally occurring, sentence-level\nprompts derived from a large corpus of English web text, paired with toxicity\nscores from a widely-used toxicity classifier. Using RealToxicityPrompts, we\nfind that pretrained LMs can degenerate into toxic text even from seemingly\ninnocuous prompts. We empirically assess several controllable generation\nmethods, and find that while data- or compute-intensive methods (e.g., adaptive\npretraining on non-toxic data) are more effective at steering away from\ntoxicity than simpler solutions (e.g., banning "bad" words), no current method\nis failsafe against neural toxic degeneration. To pinpoint the potential cause\nof such persistent toxic degeneration, we analyze two web text corpora used to\npretrain several LMs (including GPT-2; Radford et. al, 2019), and find a\nsignificant amount of offensive, factually unreliable, and otherwise toxic\ncontent. Our work provides a test bed for evaluating toxic generations by LMs\nand stresses the need for better data selection processes for pretraining.\n\n2. Technical Concept Definition from the paper RealToxicityPrompts: Evaluating Neural Toxic Degeneration in Language  Models:\n{\n  "name": "Attribute Conditioning (CTRLGPT2)",\n  "description": "A detoxification technique inspired by CTRL, where toxicity attribute tokens (e.g., <|toxic|>, <|nontoxic|>) are prepended to documents during pretraining. The model is conditioned on these tokens to steer its generations towards non-toxic text. The method involves adding these tokens to the vocabulary and continuing pretraining on a randomly sampled corpus with the appropriate attribute tokens prepended. During generation, the <|nontoxic|> token is prepended to prompts to condition the model on non-toxic text."\n}\n\n3. Sub-techniques and Associated Code:\n[\n  {\n    "name": "Attribute Token Addition",\n    "implementation": "import logging\\nfrom typing import List, Union\\n\\nfrom transformers import AutoTokenizer, PreTrainedTokenizer\\n\\nlogging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)\\nlogger = logging.getLogger(__name__)\\n\\ndef add_attribute_tokens(tokenizer: PreTrainedTokenizer, tokens: Union[str, List[str]]) -> PreTrainedTokenizer:\\n    \\"\\"\\"\\n    Adds toxicity attribute token(s) to the tokenizer\'s vocabulary to enable conditioning on toxicity levels during generation.\\n    \\n    This function checks if the provided attribute token(s) are already present in the tokenizer\'s vocabulary.\\n    If not, it adds them and updates the tokenizer accordingly. This mechanism is fundamental for models that\\n    require conditional generation based on toxicity attributes.\\n    \\n    Parameters:\\n        tokenizer (PreTrainedTokenizer): The tokenizer instance whose vocabulary will be updated.\\n        tokens (Union[str, List[str]]): A single token or list of tokens representing toxicity attributes (e.g., \\"<|nontoxic|>\\", \\"<|toxic|>\\").\\n        \\n    Returns:\\n        PreTrainedTokenizer: The updated tokenizer with the new attribute token(s) added.\\n    \\"\\"\\"\\n    # Convert single token to a list for uniform processing.\\n    if isinstance(tokens, str):\\n        tokens = [tokens]\\n\\n    initial_vocab_size = len(tokenizer)\\n    tokens_to_add = [token for token in tokens if token not in tokenizer.get_vocab()]\\n    num_tokens_added = tokenizer.add_tokens(tokens_to_add)\\n    \\n    if num_tokens_added > 0:\\n        logger.info(f\\"Added {num_tokens_added} attribute token(s): {tokens_to_add}. New vocab size: {len(tokenizer)} (was {initial_vocab_size}).\\")\\n    else:\\n        logger.info(\\"No new attribute tokens were added; all tokens already exist in the vocabulary.\\")\\n    \\n    return tokenizer",\n    "documentation": "This module implements the \\"Attribute Token Addition\\" technique which is designed to add toxicity attribute tokens to a model\'s tokenizer vocabulary. It allows for conditioning text generation on toxicity levels by integrating tokens such as \\"<|nontoxic|>\\" and \\"<|toxic|>\\" into the vocabulary. The core function, add_attribute_tokens, checks if the specified tokens exist and adds them if necessary, ensuring that the model can later recognize and condition on these attributes. The code includes proper documentation with type hints and logging for clarity, allowing for easy integration and troubleshooting. In the test block, the GPT-2 tokenizer is used as an example to demonstrate the addition of toxicity tokens, making the module self-contained and ready for execution."\n  },\n  {\n    "name": "Conditional Pretraining",\n    "implementation": "import os\\nimport pickle\\nimport time\\nimport logging\\nfrom typing import List, Any\\n\\nimport torch\\nfrom torch.utils.data import Dataset\\n\\nlogging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)\\nlogger = logging.getLogger(__name__)\\n\\n\\nclass ConditionalPretrainingDataset(Dataset):\\n    \\"\\"\\"\\n    A PyTorch Dataset for Conditional Pretraining.\\n    \\n    This dataset reads a text file, tokenizes the entire content, segments it into fixed-size blocks,\\n    and prepends each block with a specified toxicity attribute token (control token).\\n    The goal is to condition the language model during pretraining such that it generates text\\n    aligned with the specified toxicity level.\\n    \\n    Attributes:\\n        examples (List[List[int]]): List of tokenized examples with the control token prepended.\\n    \\"\\"\\"\\n\\n    def __init__(\\n        self,\\n        tokenizer: Any,\\n        file_path: str,\\n        block_size: int,\\n        ctrl_code_tok: int,\\n        overwrite_cache: bool = False,\\n        cache_dir: str = None\\n    ) -> None:\\n        \\"\\"\\"\\n        Initializes the ConditionalPretrainingDataset.\\n        \\n        Parameters:\\n            tokenizer (Any): A tokenizer instance with methods \'tokenize\', \'convert_tokens_to_ids\',\\n                             \'build_inputs_with_special_tokens\', and \'num_special_tokens_to_add\'.\\n            file_path (str): Path to the input text file.\\n            block_size (int): The size of each block (number of tokens) for training.\\n            ctrl_code_tok (int): The integer token id representing the toxicity attribute.\\n            overwrite_cache (bool): If True, ignores any cached preprocessed file and reprocesses the data.\\n            cache_dir (str): Optional directory to store the cached file. If None, uses the file directory.\\n        \\"\\"\\"\\n        if not os.path.isfile(file_path):\\n            raise ValueError(f\\"File not found: {file_path}\\")\\n        \\n        # Adjust block size to account for special tokens and the control token to be prepended.\\n        block_size = block_size - tokenizer.num_special_tokens_to_add(pair=False) - 1\\n\\n        directory = cache_dir if cache_dir is not None else os.path.dirname(file_path)\\n        filename = os.path.basename(file_path)\\n        cached_features_file = os.path.join(\\n            directory, f\\"cached_pretrain_{tokenizer.__class__.__name__}_{block_size}_{filename}\\"\\n        )\\n        self.examples: List[List[int]] = []\\n\\n        if os.path.exists(cached_features_file) and not overwrite_cache:\\n            start = time.time()\\n            with open(cached_features_file, \\"rb\\") as handle:\\n                self.examples = pickle.load(handle)\\n            logger.info(\\"Loaded cached features from %s (took %.3f seconds)\\", cached_features_file, time.time() - start)\\n        else:\\n            logger.info(\\"Creating features from dataset file at %s\\", file_path)\\n            with open(file_path, encoding=\\"utf-8\\") as f:\\n                text = f.read()\\n\\n            # Tokenize the text into ids.\\n            tokenized_text = tokenizer.convert_tokens_to_ids(tokenizer.tokenize(text))\\n\\n            # Create blocks of tokens, each prepended with the control token.\\n            for i in range(0, len(tokenized_text) - block_size + 1, block_size):\\n                block = tokenized_text[i: i + block_size]\\n                # Prepend the control token to the block\\n                example = tokenizer.build_inputs_with_special_tokens([ctrl_code_tok] + block)\\n                self.examples.append(example)\\n\\n            # Save cached features for faster future loading.\\n            start = time.time()\\n            with open(cached_features_file, \\"wb\\") as handle:\\n                pickle.dump(self.examples, handle, protocol=pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL)\\n            logger.info(\\"Saved features into cached file %s (took %.3f seconds)\\", cached_features_file, time.time() - start)\\n\\n    def __len__(self) -> int:\\n        \\"\\"\\"\\n        Returns:\\n            int: The total number of examples in the dataset.\\n        \\"\\"\\"\\n        return len(self.examples)\\n\\n    def __getitem__(self, index: int) -> torch.Tensor:\\n        \\"\\"\\"\\n        Retrieves the token ids at the specified index as a PyTorch tensor.\\n        \\n        Parameters:\\n            index (int): Index of the required example.\\n            \\n        Returns:\\n            torch.Tensor: Tensor containing token ids.\\n        \\"\\"\\"\\n        return torch.tensor(self.examples[index], dtype=torch.long)\\n\\n\\n# A simple dummy tokenizer for testing purposes.\\nclass DummyTokenizer:\\n    \\"\\"\\"\\n    A dummy tokenizer that splits text on whitespace and simulates token id conversion.\\n    This is intended solely for demonstration and testing purposes.\\n    \\"\\"\\"\\n\\n    def __init__(self) -> None:\\n        self._special_token_count = 1  # Assume one special token is added (e.g., for formatting)\\n        self._max_len = 512\\n\\n    def tokenize(self, text: str) -> List[str]:\\n        \\"\\"\\"\\n        Splits input text based on whitespace.\\n        \\n        Parameters:\\n            text (str): The input text to tokenize.\\n        \\n        Returns:\\n            List[str]: A list of token strings.\\n        \\"\\"\\"\\n        return text.strip().split()\\n\\n    def convert_tokens_to_ids(self, tokens: List[str]) -> List[int]:\\n        \\"\\"\\"\\n        Converts tokens into token ids using a simple hash-based mapping.\\n        \\n        Parameters:\\n            tokens (List[str]): List of token strings.\\n        \\n        Returns:\\n            List[int]: List of token ids.\\n        \\"\\"\\"\\n        return [abs(hash(token)) % 1000 for token in tokens]\\n\\n    def build_inputs_with_special_tokens(self, token_ids: List[int]) -> List[int]:\\n        \\"\\"\\"\\n        Constructs model inputs by simply returning the given token ids. In realistic scenarios,\\n        this would add model-specific special tokens.\\n        \\n        Parameters:\\n            token_ids (List[int]): List of token ids.\\n        \\n        Returns:\\n            List[int]: Processed list of token ids with special tokens.\\n        \\"\\"\\"\\n        return token_ids\\n\\n    def num_special_tokens_to_add(self, pair: bool = False) -> int:\\n        \\"\\"\\"\\n        Returns the number of special tokens to be added.\\n        \\n        Parameters:\\n            pair (bool): Whether the input is a pair of sentences.\\n        \\n        Returns:\\n            int: Number of special tokens.\\n        \\"\\"\\"\\n        return self._special_token_count\\n\\n    @property\\n    def max_len(self) -> int:\\n        \\"\\"\\"\\n        Returns:\\n            int: Maximum length supported by the tokenizer.\\n        \\"\\"\\"\\n        return self._max_len",\n    "documentation": "This module implements the Conditional Pretraining technique as described in the paper \\"RealToxicityPrompts: Evaluating Neural Toxic Degeneration in Language Models\\". The core functionality is encapsulated in the ConditionalPretrainingDataset class, which reads a text file, tokenizes its content, and divides it into fixed-size blocks with a specified toxicity attribute token prepended to each block. A caching mechanism is included to speed up repeated dataset loading. A DummyTokenizer class is also provided to allow easy testing without requiring an external tokenizer dependency. The TEST BLOCK at the end demonstrates how to initialize and use the dataset by processing a sample text file, making the module self-contained and executable."\n  }\n]\n\n4. Relevant Code Files:\n[{"file_name": "scripts/finetuning/finetune_gpt2_ctrl.py", "code_list": ["# coding=utf-8\\n# Copyright 2018 The Google AI Language Team Authors and The HuggingFace Inc. team.\\n# Copyright (c) 2018, NVIDIA CORPORATION.  All rights reserved.\\n#\\n# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the \\"License\\");\\n# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.\\n# You may obtain a copy of the License at\\n#\\n#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0\\n#\\n# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software\\n# distributed under the License is distributed on an \\"AS IS\\" BASIS,\\n# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.\\n# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and\\n# limitations under the License.\\n\\"\\"\\"\\nFine-tuning the library models for language modeling on a text file (GPT, GPT-2, BERT, RoBERTa).\\nGPT and GPT-2 are fine-tuned using a causal language modeling (CLM) loss while BERT and RoBERTa are fine-tuned\\nusing a masked language modeling (MLM) loss.\\n\\"\\"\\"\\n\\nimport logging\\nimport math\\nimport os\\nimport pickle\\nimport time\\nfrom dataclasses import dataclass, field\\nfrom typing import Optional, List\\n\\nimport torch\\nfrom torch.utils.data import Dataset, ConcatDataset\\nfrom tqdm import trange\\nfrom transformers import (\\n    CONFIG_MAPPING,\\n    MODEL_WITH_LM_HEAD_MAPPING,\\n    AutoConfig,\\n    AutoModelWithLMHead,\\n    AutoTokenizer,\\n    DataCollatorForLanguageModeling,\\n    HfArgumentParser,\\n    PreTrainedTokenizer,\\n    Trainer,\\n    TrainingArguments,\\n    set_seed,\\n    torch_distributed_zero_first,\\n)\\n\\nlogger = logging.getLogger(__name__)\\n\\nMODEL_CONFIG_CLASSES = list(MODEL_WITH_LM_HEAD_MAPPING.keys())\\nMODEL_TYPES = tuple(conf.model_type for conf in MODEL_CONFIG_CLASSES)\\n\\n\\nclass CTRLTextDataset(Dataset):\\n    def __init__(\\n            self, tokenizer: PreTrainedTokenizer, file_path: str, block_size: int, ctrl_code_tok: int,\\n            overwrite_cache=False, local_rank=-1,\\n    ):\\n        assert os.path.isfile(file_path)\\n\\n        # Subtract 1 so we can prepend the CTRL code\\n        block_size = block_size - tokenizer.num_special_tokens_to_add(pair=False) - 1\\n\\n        directory, filename = os.path.split(file_path)\\n        cached_features_file = os.path.join(\\n            directory, \\"cached_lm_{}_{}_{}\\".format(tokenizer.__class__.__name__, str(block_size), filename, ),\\n        )\\n\\n        with torch_distributed_zero_first(local_rank):\\n            # Make sure only the first process in distributed training processes the dataset,\\n            # and the others will use the cache.\\n\\n            if os.path.exists(cached_features_file) and not overwrite_cache:\\n                start = time.time()\\n                with open(cached_features_file, \\"rb\\") as handle:\\n                    self.examples = pickle.load(handle)\\n                logger.info(\\n                    f\\"Loading features from cached file {cached_features_file} [took %.3f s]\\", time.time() - start\\n                )\\n\\n            else:\\n                logger.info(f\\"Creating features from dataset file at {directory}\\")\\n\\n                self.examples = []\\n                with open(file_path, encoding=\\"utf-8\\") as f:\\n                    text = f.read()\\n\\n                tokenized_text = tokenizer.convert_tokens_to_ids(tokenizer.tokenize(text))\\n\\n                for i in trange(0, len(tokenized_text) - block_size + 1, block_size):  # Truncate in block of block_size\\n                    self.examples.append(\\n                        tokenizer.build_inputs_with_special_tokens([ctrl_code_tok] + tokenized_text[i: i + block_size])\\n                    )\\n                # Note that we are losing the last truncated example here for the sake of simplicity (no padding)\\n                # If your dataset is small, first you should loook for a bigger one :-) and second you\\n                # can change this behavior by adding (model specific) padding.\\n\\n                start = time.time()\\n                with open(cached_features_file, \\"wb\\") as handle:\\n                    pickle.dump(self.examples, handle, protocol=pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL)\\n                logger.info(\\n                    f\\"Saving features into cached file %s [took %.3f s]\\", cached_features_file, time.time() - start\\n                )\\n\\n    def __len__(self):\\n        return len(self.examples)\\n\\n    def __getitem__(self, i) -> torch.Tensor:\\n        return torch.tensor(self.examples[i], dtype=torch.long)\\n\\n\\n@dataclass\\nclass ModelArguments:\\n    \\"\\"\\"\\n    Arguments pertaining to which model/config/tokenizer we are going to fine-tune, or train from scratch.\\n    \\"\\"\\"\\n\\n    model_name_or_path: Optional[str] = field(\\n        default=None,\\n        metadata={\\n            \\"help\\": \\"The model checkpoint for weights initialization. Leave None if you want to train a model from scratch.\\"\\n        },\\n    )\\n    model_type: Optional[str] = field(\\n        default=None,\\n        metadata={\\"help\\": \\"If training from scratch, pass a model type from the list: \\" + \\", \\".join(MODEL_TYPES)},\\n    )\\n    config_name: Optional[str] = field(\\n        default=None, metadata={\\"help\\": \\"Pretrained config name or path if not the same as model_name\\"}\\n    )\\n    tokenizer_name: Optional[str] = field(\\n        default=None, metadata={\\"help\\": \\"Pretrained tokenizer name or path if not the same as model_name\\"}\\n    )\\n    cache_dir: Optional[str] = field(\\n        default=None, metadata={\\"help\\": \\"Where do you want to store the pretrained models downloaded from s3\\"}\\n    )\\n\\n\\n@dataclass\\nclass DataTrainingArguments:\\n    \\"\\"\\"\\n    Arguments pertaining to what data we are going to input our model for training and eval.\\n    \\"\\"\\"\\n\\n    train_data_files: Optional[str] = field(\\n        default=None,\\n        metadata={\\n            \\"nargs\\": \\"+\\",\\n        }\\n    )\\n    eval_data_files: Optional[str] = field(\\n        default=None,\\n        metadata={\\n            \\"nargs\\": \\"+\\",\\n        },\\n    )\\n    ctrl_codes: Optional[str] = field(\\n        default=None,\\n        metadata={\\n            \\"nargs\\": \\"+\\"\\n        }\\n    )\\n\\n    mlm: bool = field(\\n        default=False, metadata={\\"help\\": \\"Train with masked-language modeling loss instead of language modeling.\\"}\\n    )\\n    mlm_probability: float = field(\\n        default=0.15, metadata={\\"help\\": \\"Ratio of tokens to mask for masked language modeling loss\\"}\\n    )\\n\\n    block_size: int = field(\\n        default=-1,\\n        metadata={\\n            \\"help\\": \\"Optional input sequence length after tokenization.\\"\\n                    \\"The training dataset will be truncated in block of this size for training.\\"\\n                    \\"Default to the model max input length for single sentence inputs (take into account special tokens).\\"\\n        },\\n    )\\n    overwrite_cache: bool = field(\\n        default=False, metadata={\\"help\\": \\"Overwrite the cached training and evaluation sets\\"}\\n    )\\n\\n\\ndef get_dataset(args: DataTrainingArguments, tokenizer: PreTrainedTokenizer, ctrl_codes: List[int], evaluate=False,\\n                local_rank=-1):\\n    file_paths = args.eval_data_files if evaluate else args.train_data_files\\n    datasets = []\\n    for file_path, ctrl_code in zip(file_paths, ctrl_codes):\\n        d = CTRLTextDataset(tokenizer=tokenizer, file_path=file_path, block_size=args.block_size,\\n                            ctrl_code_tok=ctrl_code, local_rank=local_rank)\\n        datasets.append(d)\\n    return ConcatDataset(datasets)\\n\\n\\ndef main():\\n    # See all possible arguments in src/transformers/training_args.py\\n    # or by passing the --help flag to this script.\\n    # We now keep distinct sets of args, for a cleaner separation of concerns.\\n\\n    parser = HfArgumentParser((ModelArguments, DataTrainingArguments, TrainingArguments))\\n    model_args, data_args, training_args = parser.parse_args_into_dataclasses()\\n\\n    if data_args.eval_data_files is None and training_args.do_eval:\\n        raise ValueError(\\n            \\"Cannot do evaluation without an evaluation data file. Either supply a file to --eval_data_file \\"\\n            \\"or remove the --do_eval argument.\\"\\n        )\\n\\n    if (\\n            os.path.exists(training_args.output_dir)\\n            and os.listdir(training_args.output_dir)\\n            and training_args.do_train\\n            and not training_args.overwrite_output_dir\\n    ):\\n        raise ValueError(\\n            f\\"Output directory ({training_args.output_dir}) already exists and is not empty. Use --overwrite_output_dir to overcome.\\"\\n        )\\n\\n    # Setup logging\\n    logging.basicConfig(\\n        format=\\"%(asctime)s - %(levelname)s - %(name)s -   %(message)s\\",\\n        datefmt=\\"%m/%d/%Y %H:%M:%S\\",\\n        level=logging.INFO if training_args.local_rank in [-1, 0] else logging.WARN,\\n    )\\n    logger.warning(\\n        \\"Process rank: %s, device: %s, n_gpu: %s, distributed training: %s, 16-bits training: %s\\",\\n        training_args.local_rank,\\n        training_args.device,\\n        training_args.n_gpu,\\n        bool(training_args.local_rank != -1),\\n        training_args.fp16,\\n    )\\n    logger.info(\\"Training/evaluation parameters %s\\", training_args)\\n\\n    # Set seed\\n    set_seed(training_args.seed)\\n\\n    # Load pretrained model and tokenizer\\n    #\\n    # Distributed training:\\n    # The .from_pretrained methods guarantee that only one local process can concurrently\\n    # download model & vocab.\\n\\n    if model_args.config_name:\\n        config = AutoConfig.from_pretrained(model_args.config_name, cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir)\\n    elif model_args.model_name_or_path:\\n        config = AutoConfig.from_pretrained(model_args.model_name_or_path, cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir)\\n    else:\\n        config = CONFIG_MAPPING[model_args.model_type]()\\n        logger.warning(\\"You are instantiating a new config instance from scratch.\\")\\n\\n    if model_args.tokenizer_name:\\n        tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_args.tokenizer_name, cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir)\\n    elif model_args.model_name_or_path:\\n        tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_args.model_name_or_path, cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir)\\n    else:\\n        raise ValueError(\\n            \\"You are instantiating a new tokenizer from scratch. This is not supported, but you can do it from another script, save it,\\"\\n            \\"and load it from here, using --tokenizer_name\\"\\n        )\\n\\n    # Add CTRL codes to vocab\\n    assert len(data_args.ctrl_codes) == len(data_args.eval_data_files) == len(data_args.ctrl_codes)\\n    print(\\"CTRL codes:\\", *data_args.ctrl_codes)\\n    num_added_toks = tokenizer.add_tokens(data_args.ctrl_codes)\\n    assert num_added_toks == len(data_args.ctrl_codes)\\n    ctrl_code_toks = tokenizer.encode(data_args.ctrl_codes)\\n\\n    if model_args.model_name_or_path:\\n        model = AutoModelWithLMHead.from_pretrained(\\n            model_args.model_name_or_path,\\n            from_tf=bool(\\".ckpt\\" in model_args.model_name_or_path),\\n            config=config,\\n            cache_dir=model_args.cache_dir,\\n        )\\n    else:\\n        logger.info(\\"Training new model from scratch\\")\\n        model = AutoModelWithLMHead.from_config(config)\\n\\n    model.resize_token_embeddings(len(tokenizer))\\n\\n    if config.model_type in [\\"bert\\", \\"roberta\\", \\"distilbert\\", \\"camembert\\"] and not data_args.mlm:\\n        raise ValueError(\\n            \\"BERT and RoBERTa-like models do not have LM heads but masked LM heads. They must be run using the --mlm \\"\\n            \\"flag (masked language modeling).\\"\\n        )\\n\\n    if data_args.block_size <= 0:\\n        data_args.block_size = tokenizer.max_len\\n        # Our input block size will be the max possible for the model\\n    else:\\n        data_args.block_size = min(data_args.block_size, tokenizer.max_len)\\n\\n    # Get datasets\\n    train_dataset = (\\n        get_dataset(data_args, tokenizer=tokenizer, local_rank=training_args.local_rank, ctrl_codes=ctrl_code_toks)\\n        if training_args.do_train\\n        else None\\n    )\\n    eval_dataset = (\\n        get_dataset(data_args, tokenizer=tokenizer, local_rank=training_args.local_rank, evaluate=True, ctrl_codes=ctrl_code_toks)\\n        if training_args.do_eval\\n        else None\\n    )\\n    data_collator = DataCollatorForLanguageModeling(\\n        tokenizer=tokenizer, mlm=data_args.mlm, mlm_probability=data_args.mlm_probability\\n    )\\n\\n    # Initialize our Trainer\\n    trainer = Trainer(\\n        model=model,\\n        args=training_args,\\n        data_collator=data_collator,\\n        train_dataset=train_dataset,\\n        eval_dataset=eval_dataset,\\n        prediction_loss_only=True,\\n    )\\n\\n    # Training\\n    if training_args.do_train:\\n        model_path = (\\n            model_args.model_name_or_path\\n            if model_args.model_name_or_path is not None and os.path.isdir(model_args.model_name_or_path)\\n            else None\\n        )\\n        trainer.train(model_path=model_path)\\n        trainer.save_model()\\n        # For convenience, we also re-save the tokenizer to the same directory,\\n        # so that you can share your model easily on huggingface.co/models =)\\n        if trainer.is_world_master():\\n            tokenizer.save_pretrained(training_args.output_dir)\\n\\n    # import logging
import os
import pickle
import time
from typing import List, Union, Any

import torch
from torch.utils.data import Dataset

# Setup logging
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

def add_attribute_tokens(tokenizer: Any, tokens: Union[str, List[str]]) -> Any:
    """
    Adds toxicity attribute token(s) to the tokenizer's vocabulary to enable conditioning on toxicity levels during generation.
    
    This function checks if the provided attribute token(s) are already present in the tokenizer's vocabulary.
    If not, it adds them and updates the tokenizer accordingly.
    
    Parameters:
        tokenizer (Any): The tokenizer instance whose vocabulary will be updated. Must support get_vocab() and add_tokens().
        tokens (Union[str, List[str]]): A single token or list of tokens representing toxicity attributes.
        
    Returns:
        Any: The updated tokenizer with the new attribute token(s) added.
    """
    # Convert a single token to a list for uniform processing.
    if isinstance(tokens, str):
        tokens = [tokens]
    
    initial_vocab_size = len(tokenizer)
    tokens_to_add = [token for token in tokens if token not in tokenizer.get_vocab()]
    num_tokens_added = tokenizer.add_tokens(tokens_to_add)
    
    if num_tokens_added > 0:
        logger.info(f"Added {num_tokens_added} attribute token(s): {tokens_to_add}. New vocab size: {len(tokenizer)} (was {initial_vocab_size}).")
    else:
        logger.info("No new attribute tokens were added; all tokens already exist in the vocabulary.")
    
    return tokenizer

class ConditionalPretrainingDataset(Dataset):
    """
    A PyTorch Dataset for Conditional Pretraining.

    This dataset reads a text file, tokenizes the entire content, segments it into fixed-size blocks,
    and prepends each block with a specified toxicity attribute token (control token). The goal is to 
    condition the language model during pretraining such that it generates text aligned with the specified toxicity level.

    Attributes:
        examples (List[List[int]]): List of tokenized examples with the control token prepended.
    """
    def __init__(
        self,
        tokenizer: Any,
        file_path: str,
        block_size: int,
        ctrl_code_tok: int,
        overwrite_cache: bool = False,
        cache_dir: str = None
    ) -> None:
        """
        Initializes the ConditionalPretrainingDataset.

        Parameters:
            tokenizer (Any): A tokenizer instance supporting tokenize, convert_tokens_to_ids, 
                             build_inputs_with_special_tokens, and num_special_tokens_to_add.
            file_path (str): Path to the input text file.
            block_size (int): The size of each block (number of tokens) for training.
            ctrl_code_tok (int): The integer token id representing the toxicity attribute.
            overwrite_cache (bool): If True, ignores any cached preprocessed file and reprocesses the data.
            cache_dir (str): Optional directory to store the cached file. If None, uses the file directory.
        """
        if not os.path.isfile(file_path):
            raise ValueError(f"File not found: {file_path}")
        
        # Adjust block size to account for special tokens and the control token to be prepended.
        block_size = block_size - tokenizer.num_special_tokens_to_add(pair=False) - 1

        directory = cache_dir if cache_dir is not None else os.path.dirname(file_path)
        filename = os.path.basename(file_path)
        cached_features_file = os.path.join(
            directory, f"cached_pretrain_{tokenizer.__class__.__name__}_{block_size}_{filename}"
        )
        self.examples: List[List[int]] = []

        if os.path.exists(cached_features_file) and not overwrite_cache:
            start = time.time()
            with open(cached_features_file, "rb") as handle:
                self.examples = pickle.load(handle)
            logger.info("Loaded cached features from %s (took %.3f seconds)", cached_features_file, time.time() - start)
        else:
            logger.info("Creating features from dataset file at %s", file_path)
            with open(file_path, encoding="utf-8") as f:
                text = f.read()

            # Tokenize the text into ids.
            tokenized_text = tokenizer.convert_tokens_to_ids(tokenizer.tokenize(text))

            # Create blocks of tokens, each prepended with the control token.
            for i in range(0, len(tokenized_text) - block_size + 1, block_size):
                block = tokenized_text[i: i + block_size]
                # Prepend the control token to the block.
                example = tokenizer.build_inputs_with_special_tokens([ctrl_code_tok] + block)
                self.examples.append(example)

            # Save cached features for faster future loading.
            start = time.time()
            with open(cached_features_file, "wb") as handle:
                pickle.dump(self.examples, handle, protocol=pickle.HIGHEST_PROTOCOL)
            logger.info("Saved features into cached file %s (took %.3f seconds)", cached_features_file, time.time() - start)

    def __len__(self) -> int:
        """
        Returns:
            int: The total number of examples in the dataset.
        """
        return len(self.examples)

    def __getitem__(self, index: int) -> torch.Tensor:
        """
        Retrieves the token ids at the specified index as a PyTorch tensor.

        Parameters:
            index (int): Index of the required example.

        Returns:
            torch.Tensor: Tensor containing token ids.
        """
        return torch.tensor(self.examples[index], dtype=torch.long)

class DummyTokenizer:
    """
    A dummy tokenizer that splits text on whitespace and simulates token id conversion.
    This is intended solely for demonstration and testing purposes.
    """
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._special_token_count = 1  # Assume one special token is added (e.g., for formatting)
        self._max_len = 512
        self._vocab = {"<|nontoxic|>": 1001, "<|toxic|>": 1002}  # Predefined tokens in vocab

    def tokenize(self, text: str) -> List[str]:
        """
        Splits input text based on whitespace.

        Parameters:
            text (str): The input text to tokenize.

        Returns:
            List[str]: A list of token strings.
        """
        return text.strip().split()

    def convert_tokens_to_ids(self, tokens: List[str]) -> List[int]:
        """
        Converts tokens into token ids using a simple hash-based mapping if not in the vocab.

        Parameters:
            tokens (List[str]): List of token strings.

        Returns:
            List[int]: List of token ids.
        """
        token_ids = []
        for token in tokens:
            if token in self._vocab:
                token_ids.append(self._vocab[token])
            else:
                # Simple conversion: hash mod 1000
                token_ids.append(abs(hash(token)) % 1000)
        return token_ids

    def build_inputs_with_special_tokens(self, token_ids: List[int]) -> List[int]:
        """
        Constructs model inputs. In a realistic scenario, this would add model-specific special tokens.
        
        Parameters:
            token_ids (List[int]): List of token ids.

        Returns:
            List[int]: Processed list of token ids with any special tokens.
        """
        return token_ids

    def num_special_tokens_to_add(self, pair: bool = False) -> int:
        """
        Returns the number of special tokens to be added.

        Parameters:
            pair (bool): Whether the input is a pair of sentences.

        Returns:
            int: Number of special tokens.
        """
        return self._special_token_count

    def get_vocab(self) -> dict:
        """
        Returns the vocabulary dictionary.

        Returns:
            dict: The tokenizer's vocabulary.
        """
        return self._vocab

    def add_tokens(self, tokens: List[str]) -> int:
        """
        Adds new tokens to the vocabulary.

        Parameters:
            tokens (List[str]): List of tokens to be added.

        Returns:
            int: Number of tokens actually added.
        """
        added = 0
        for token in tokens:
            if token not in self._vocab:
                # Add token with id as next available integer (max id + 1)
                new_id = max(self._vocab.values()) + 1
                self._vocab[token] = new_id
                added += 1
        return added

    @property
    def max_len(self) -> int:
        """
        Returns:
            int: Maximum length supported by the tokenizer.
        """
        return self._max_len

    def __len__(self) -> int:
        """
        Returns:
            int: The size of the vocabulary.
        """
        return len(self._vocab)

# TEST BLOCK
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Create a dummy tokenizer instance.
    tokenizer = DummyTokenizer()
    
    # Define attribute tokens as specified in the technique.
    attribute_tokens = ["<|nontoxic|>", "<|toxic|>"]
    
    # Add attribute tokens to the tokenizer.
    tokenizer = add_attribute_tokens(tokenizer, attribute_tokens)
    
    # Create a temporary text file for demonstration.
    sample_text = ("This is a sample sentence used to test conditional pretraining. " 
                   "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. " * 5)
    temp_file = "sample_text.txt"
    with open(temp_file, "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        f.write(sample_text)
        
    # Select control token id (using the non-toxic token) for conditioning.
    ctrl_token = "<|nontoxic|>"
    ctrl_token_id = tokenizer.get_vocab()[ctrl_token]
    
    # Define block size (for testing, choose a small block size)
    block_size = 20
    
    # Create the ConditionalPretrainingDataset.
    dataset = ConditionalPretrainingDataset(
        tokenizer=tokenizer,
        file_path=temp_file,
        block_size=block_size,
        ctrl_code_tok=ctrl_token_id,
        overwrite_cache=True
    )
    
    # Display the number of examples and one sample output.
    logger.info("Number of examples in the dataset: %d", len(dataset))
    sample_example = dataset[0]
    logger.info("Sample token ids from the first example: %s", sample_example.tolist())
    
    # Cleanup the temporary file.
    os.remove(temp_file)
```

```
This module implements the Attribute Conditioning (CTRLGPT2) technique for detoxifying neural language model outputs. It includes two main components: the add_attribute_tokens function for adding toxicity attribute tokens to a tokenizer's vocabulary, and the ConditionalPretrainingDataset class for preparing text data by prepending control tokens during pretraining. A DummyTokenizer is provided for testing purposes, simulating basic tokenization and vocabulary management. The code is structured to allow easy integration, with detailed docstrings and type hints for clarity. In the TEST BLOCK, a small sample text file is created, and the dataset is constructed using the non-toxic attribute token to illustrate the conditional pretraining preparation. Configurable parameters such as block size, overwrite cache, and token values are clearly defined for reproducibility.

# Task
Your task is to transform a collection of disparate source code snippets, which are the official implementation of a technique component from a research paper RealToxicityPrompts: Evaluating Neural Toxic Degeneration in Language  Models, into a single, self-contained, and executable code block. # Input
1. Abstract of the paper RealToxicityPrompts: Evaluating Neural Toxic Degeneration in Language  Models:
Pretrained neural language models (LMs) are prone to generating racist,
sexist, or otherwise toxic language which hinders their safe deployment. We
investigate the extent to which pretrained LMs can be prompted to generate
toxic language, and the effectiveness of controllable text generation
algorithms at preventing such toxic degeneration. We create and release
RealToxicityPrompts, a dataset of 100K naturally occurring, sentence-level
prompts derived from a large corpus of English web text, paired with toxicity
scores from a widely-used toxicity classifier. Using RealToxicityPrompts, we
find that pretrained LMs can degenerate into toxic text even from seemingly
innocuous prompts. We empirically assess several controllable generation
methods, and find that while data- or compute-intensive methods (e.g., adaptive
pretraining on non-toxic data) are more effective at steering away from
toxicity than simpler solutions (e.g., banning "bad" words), no current method
is failsafe against neural toxic degeneration. To pinpoint the potential cause
of such persistent toxic degeneration, we analyze two web text corpora used to
pretrain several LMs (including GPT-2; Radford et. al, 2019), and find a
significant amount of offensive, factually unreliable, and otherwise toxic
content. Our work provides a test bed for evaluating toxic generations by LMs
and stresses the need for better data selection processes for pretraining.

2. Technical Concept Definition from the paper RealToxicityPrompts: Evaluating Neural Toxic Degeneration in Language  Models:
{
  "name": "Vocabulary Shifting (AffectGPT2)",
  "description": "A decoding-based detoxification technique that learns a 2-dimensional representation of toxicity and non-toxicity for every token in the model's vocabulary. This representation is used to boost the likelihood of non-toxic tokens during generation. The method involves training a projection layer on a balanced subsample of non-toxic and toxic corpora, then using this layer to adjust the model's vocabulary logits during generation by adding a term that encodes the desired toxicity level."
}

3. Sub-techniques and Associated Code:
[
  {
    "name": "Toxicity Representation Learning",
    "implementation": "import torch\nimport torch.nn as nn\nfrom typing import List\n\nclass ToxicityRepresentation(nn.Module):\n    \"\"\"\n    Implements a toxicity representation learning module that learns a 2-dimensional representation\n    for toxicity (toxic vs. non-toxic) for each token in the vocabulary. This module projects\n    token-level 2-dimensional affect features into vocabulary logits via a linear transformation.\n    A configurable scaling factor 'beta' is applied to the logits, and there is an option to ignore\n    specific special tokens by zeroing their logits.\n    \"\"\"\n    \n    IGNORE_IDS: List[int] = [50256, 247, 251, 250]\n\n    def __init__(self, vocab_size: int, beta: float = 1.0, ignore_special_tokens: bool = False) -> None:\n        \"\"\"\n        Initializes the toxicity representation module.\n\n        Args:\n            vocab_size (int): The size of the token vocabulary.\n            beta (float): Scaling factor applied to the output logits.\n            ignore_special_tokens (bool): If True, zero out logits for tokens specified in IGNORE_IDS.\n        \"\"\"\n        super().__init__()\n        # The affect dimension is fixed to 2 (toxic vs. non-toxic)\n        self.beta = beta\n        self.ignore_special_tokens = ignore_special_tokens\n        self.affect2vocab = nn.Linear(2, vocab_size, bias=True)\n        self.init_weights()\n\n    def init_weights(self, initializer_range: float = 0.02) -> None:\n        \"\"\"\n        Initializes the weights of the linear layer using a normal distribution.\n\n        Args:\n            initializer_range (float): Standard deviation for weight initialization.\n        \"\"\"\n        self.affect2vocab.weight.data.normal_(mean=0.0, std=initializer_range)\n        self.affect2vocab.bias.data.zero_()\n\n    def forward(self, affect_labels: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:\n        \"\"\"\n        Performs a forward pass through the module to compute vocabulary logits\n        based on the 2-dimensional affect features.\n\n        Args:\n            affect_labels (torch.Tensor): A tensor of shape (batch_size, seq_length, 2)\n                                          representing token-level toxicity features.\n\n        Returns:\n            torch.Tensor: A tensor of shape (batch_size, seq_length, vocab_size) containing\n                          the scaled logits.\n        \"\"\"\n        logits = self.affect2vocab(affect_labels)\n        if self.ignore_special_tokens:\n            # Zero out logits for specific special tokens across the last dimension\n            logits[..., self.IGNORE_IDS] = 0\n        return self.beta * logits",
    "documentation": "This module implements the core logic of Toxicity Representation Learning by mapping 2-dimensional token-level affect features—representing toxic and non-toxic attributes—to vocabulary logits. It uses a linear layer to project the 2-dimensional representation into the vocabulary space, applying a configurable scaling factor beta to allow for fine-tuning the output magnitude. The module also includes an option to ignore specific special tokens by zeroing their logits, which can be helpful in managing tokens that otherwise might skew results. Clear type hints and comprehensive docstrings ensure that the code is easily understandable and maintainable. The accompanying test block demonstrates how to instantiate and execute the module using dummy data, making it straightforward to integrate into larger projects for toxicity analysis."
  }

4. Relevant Code Files:
[{"file_name": "models/affect_lm.py", "code_list": ["from typing import Optional\n\nimport torch\nfrom torch import nn\nfrom torch.nn import CrossEntropyLoss\nfrom transformers import GPT2LMHeadModel\n\nNUM_AFFECTS = 2\n\n\nclass Affect(nn.Module):\n    \"\"\"\n    See section 4.3 of Ghosh:\n        Affect-LM can also be used as a language model where the next predicted word is\n        estimated from the words in the context, along with an affect category extracted\n        from the context words themselves (instead of being encoded externally as in generation).\n\n    beta in [1.0, 1.5, 1.75, 2.0, 2.25, 2.5, 3.0]\n    \"\"\"\n\n    IGNORE_IDS = [50256, 247, 251, 250]\n\n    def __init__(self,\n                 affect_dim: int,\n                 vocab_size: int,\n                 beta: float = 1.0,\n                 ignore_special_tokens=False):\n        super().__init__()\n        self.beta = beta\n        self.ignore_special_tokens = ignore_special_tokens\n        self.affect2vocab = nn.Linear(affect_dim, vocab_size, bias=True)\n        self.init_weights()\n\n    def forward(self, affect_labels: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:\n        out = self.affect2vocab(affect_labels)\n        if self.ignore_special_tokens:\n            out[:, :, self.IGNORE_IDS] = 0\n        return self.beta * out\n\n    def init_weights(self, initializer_range=0.02):\n        # initializer_range taken from GPT2Config\n        self.affect2vocab.weight.data.normal_(mean=0.0, std=initializer_range)\n        self.affect2vocab.bias.data.zero_()\n\n\nclass AffectGPT2LMHeadModel(GPT2LMHeadModel):\n    affect_labels: Optional[torch.Tensor]\n\n    def __init__(self, config):\n        super().__init__(config)\n        self.affect = Affect(NUM_AFFECTS, config.vocab_size)\n        self.affect_labels = None\n\n    def freeze_transformer(self):\n        for p in self.transformer.parameters():\n            p.requires_grad = False\n\n    def freeze_lm_head(self):\n        for p in self.lm_head.parameters():\n            p.requires_grad = False\n\n    def set_affect_labels(self, affect_labels: torch.Tensor):\n        print(\"Using static affect labels:\", affect_labels)\n        self.affect_labels = affect_labels\n\n    def forward(\n            self,\n            input_ids=None,\n            past=None,\n            attention_mask=None,\n            token_type_ids=None,\n            position_ids=None,\n            head_mask=None,\n            inputs_embeds=None,\n            labels=None,\n            affect_labels=None,\n    ):\n        transformer_outputs = self.transformer(\n            input_ids,\n            past=past,\n            attention_mask=attention_mask,\n            token_type_ids=token_type_ids,\n            position_ids=position_ids,\n            head_mask=head_mask,\n            inputs_embeds=inputs_embeds,\n        )\n        hidden_states = transformer_outputs[0]\n\n        lm_logits = self.lm_head(hidden_states)\n\n        if affect_labels is None and self.affect_labels is not None:\n            affect_labels = self.affect_labels.repeat(len(input_ids), 1, 1)\n\n        if affect_labels is not None and self.affect is not None:\n            # Add affect logits to lm logits\n            affect_logits = self.affect(affect_labels)\n            lm_logits += affect_logits\n\n        outputs = (lm_logits,) + transformer_outputs[1:]\n        if labels is not None:\n            # Shift so that tokens < n predict n\n            shift_logits = lm_logits[..., :-1, :].contiguous()\n            shift_labels = labels[..., 1:].contiguous()\n            # Flatten the tokens\n            loss_fct = CrossEntropyLoss()\n            loss = loss_fct(shift_logits.view(-1, shift_logits.size(-1)), shift_labels.view(-1))\n            outputs = (loss,) + outputs\n\n        return outputs  # (loss), lm_logits, presents, (all hidden_states), (attentions)\n"]}, {"file_name": "scripts/finetuning/finetune_affect_lm.py", "code_list": ["# coding=utf-8\n# Copyright 2018 The Google AI Language Team Authors and The HuggingFace Inc. team.\n# Copyright (c) 2018, NVIDIA CORPORATION. Abstract of the paper RealToxicityPrompts: Evaluating Neural Toxic Degeneration in Language  Models:\nPretrained neural language models (LMs) are prone to generating racist,\nsexist, or otherwise toxic language which hinders their safe deployment. We\ninvestigate the extent to which pretrained LMs can be prompted to generate\ntoxic language, and the effectiveness of controllable text generation\nalgorithms at preventing such toxic degeneration. We create and release\nRealToxicityPrompts, a dataset of 100K naturally occurring, sentence-level\nprompts derived from a large corpus of English web text, paired with toxicity\nscores from a widely-used toxicity classifier. Using RealToxicityPrompts, we\nfind that pretrained LMs can degenerate into toxic text even from seemingly\ninnocuous prompts. We empirically assess several controllable generation\nmethods, and find that while data- or compute-intensive methods (e.g., adaptive\npretraining on non-toxic data) are more effective at steering away from\ntoxicity than simpler solutions (e.g., banning "bad" words), no current method\nis failsafe against neural toxic degeneration. To pinpoint the potential cause\nof such persistent toxic degeneration, we analyze two web text corpora used to\npretrain several LMs (including GPT-2; Radford et. al, 2019), and find a\nsignificant amount of offensive, factually unreliable, and otherwise toxic\ncontent. Our work provides a test bed for evaluating toxic generations by LMs\nand stresses the need for better data selection processes for pretraining.\n\n2. 2025-09-18 23:38:27,597 - llm.llm_def - DEBUG - OpenAI query completed in 16.76s Response: ```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from typing import List

class ToxicityRepresentation(nn.Module):
    """
    Implements the Vocabulary Shifting (AffectGPT2) technique by learning a 2-dimensional
    representation (toxic vs. non-toxic) for each token in the vocabulary and projecting it into
    vocabulary logits using a linear transformation. A scaling factor 'beta' is applied to adjust the
    magnitude of the logits, and an optional mechanism is provided to ignore specific special tokens.
    
    This module is designed to be easily integrated in language generation pipelines where the toxic
    tendencies of a model need to be mitigated by boosting non-toxic token probabilities.
    """
    
    # By default, the following special token IDs are ignored when computing vocab logits.
    IGNORE_IDS: List[int] = [50256, 247, 251, 250]

    def __init__(self, vocab_size: int, beta: float = 1.0, ignore_special_tokens: bool = False) -> None:
        """
        Initializes the ToxicityRepresentation module.
        
        Args:
            vocab_size (int): The number of tokens in the vocabulary.
            beta (float, optional): Scaling factor to adjust the output logits. Defaults to 1.0.
            ignore_special_tokens (bool, optional): If True, logits for tokens in IGNORE_IDS are zeroed out.
                                                    Defaults to False.
        """
        super().__init__()
        self.beta = beta
        self.ignore_special_tokens = ignore_special_tokens
        # A linear projection from a 2-dimensional affect space to the vocabulary space.
        self.affect2vocab = nn.Linear(2, vocab_size, bias=True)
        self.init_weights()

    def init_weights(self, initializer_range: float = 0.02) -> None:
        """
        Initializes the weights of the linear projection layer using a normal distribution.
        
        Args:
            initializer_range (float, optional): Standard deviation for weight initialization.
                                                   Defaults to 0.02.
        """
        self.affect2vocab.weight.data.normal_(mean=0.0, std=initializer_range)
        self.affect2vocab.bias.data.zero_()

    def forward(self, affect_labels: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """
        Computes the vocabulary logits by projecting 2-dimensional affect features and
        applying the scaling factor. Optionally zeroes logits for specified special tokens.
        
        Args:
            affect_labels (torch.Tensor): Tensor of shape (batch_size, seq_length, 2) representing
                                          token-level toxicity features.
        
        Returns:
            torch.Tensor: Adjusted vocabulary logits of shape (batch_size, seq_length, vocab_size).
        """
        logits = self.affect2vocab(affect_labels)
        if self.ignore_special_tokens:
            logits[..., self.IGNORE_IDS] = 0
        return self.beta * logits

# TEST BLOCK
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Define dummy parameters for testing the module.
    batch_size = 2
    seq_length = 5
    vocab_size = 10000  # Example vocabulary size.
    beta = 1.5
    ignore_special_tokens = True

    # Create a dummy tensor for affect labels with shape (batch_size, seq_length, 2).
    dummy_affect_labels = torch.randn(batch_size, seq_length, 2)

    # Instantiate the ToxicityRepresentation module.
    toxicity_module = ToxicityRepresentation(vocab_size=vocab_size, beta=beta, ignore_special_tokens=ignore_special_tokens)

    # Perform a forward pass to compute the adjusted vocabulary logits.
    output_logits = toxicity_module(dummy_affect_labels)

    # Print the output shape and a sample of the computed logits.
    print("Output logits shape:", output_logits.shape)
    print("Sample output logits (first 10 values of first token):", output_logits[0, 0, :10])
```

```
This module implements the Vocabulary Shifting (AffectGPT2) technique for detoxification in neural language models. It learns a 2-dimensional representation of each token’s affect properties (toxic vs. non-toxic) and projects these features into vocabulary logits. The scaling factor beta enables control over the magnitude of the projected logits, facilitating fine-grained adjustments during generation. Additionally, the option to ignore specific special tokens ensures that designated tokens are not adversely affected during detoxification. The code is self-contained, fully documented with comprehensive type hints, and includes a test block demonstrating its usage with dummy data.
